EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably strong winds occurred for a third straight day.  The majority of the high winds occurred along the Colorado/Kansas border, with more sporadic damage and/or high wind gusts to the east.  Noteworthy damage reports include three inch diameter tree limbs being blown down in St. Francis which may have also caused power poles to be broken, the high winds pushing two semis together on I-70 near Kanorado, and a truck being flipped that was towing a trailer near Lenora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was measured at seven N of Ludell.

Unorganized supercells formed along the dry line in Wallace/Logan counties and moved east into an increasingly unstable air mass. As the cells moved east, they merged into a line that extended from Greeley County to Sheridan County. The storms from this event produced a landspout tornado near Hoxie, KS, up to ping pong sized hail, and flash flooding in Grinnell, KS with a vehicle stuck in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gove County Dispatch reported approximately 10 inches of water under the I-70 overpass at mile marker 86. A vehicle was reported to be stuck in the water. Flash flooding occurred due to training of cells within a line moving NE and then the line pushed east out of the area around an hour later. Damage costs to the vehicle are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unorganized supercells formed along the dry line in Wallace/Logan counties and moved east into an increasingly unstable air mass. As the cells moved east, they merged into a line that extended from Greeley County to Sheridan County. The storms from this event produced a landspout tornado near Hoxie, KS, up to ping pong sized hail, and flash flooding in Grinnell, KS with a vehicle stuck in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A well documented landspout tornado occurred in Sheridan County. Numerous calls and reports were received. However, looking at the photographic and radar evidence, this appears to be one long-lived and slow moving landspout. A center pivot was overturned in a field and this is the only known damage from this event. As a result, an EF-0 rating was assigned with winds likely around 80 mph with this particular tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status for Greeley and Wichita counties largely remained unchanged.  The D3 drought status expended eastward some into Greeley County, while Wichita County remained unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month was on either side of two inches for the county.  This was 0.93 to 0.81 below normal for the month.  The southwest corner of the county worsened to D3 drought during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved south into Northwest Kansas during the evening.  Wind gusts up to 60 MPH were recorded as the storms moved through, with the strongest gust occurring near St. Francis.  Hail up to two inches in diameter fell with these storms, with the largest hail reported near Herndon.  As the storms moved south their intensity declined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated time of report from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of stationary thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall over Dundy County Nebraska expanded south into Cheyenne County.  Rainfall amounts of over eight inches fell, with the highest amounts of rain occurring near the Nebraska border.  Multiple roads had sections washed out, some as deep as 10 ft or so.  Some farms in the northern part of the county were stranded due to all the roads leading out being washed out.  Water in the Republican River was running so fast and deep that trees were snapping.  One bridge in the county was damaged and another was washed out from the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the road damage from flooding occurred north of CR S, with the damage becoming more severe toward the Nebraska border. One bridge was partially damaged east of Highway 27 near CR Z. Another bridge was washed out at River Road and RS 115. A culvert was washed out on CR 19 between CR AA and CR BB. Near CR BB and CR 17 a barb wire fence was washed onto the road. Near CR 15 and CR V flood waters had washed a large tree onto the road. Near CR 2 the Republican River was out of its banks. Hackberry Creek was also flooding where it joined with the Republican River. Damage from the flooding is unknown due to the unknown number of sections of road washed out and the unknown acres of crop land flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought t-storms to the area on the morning of Friday January 10th.  Rain and T-storms turned to freezing rain as much colder air surged southward by late morning. Ice accumulation of one tenth and up to around one quarter of an inch occurred mainly from mid morning to late afternoon on Jan 10th.  In addition northeast winds of 20 to 30 mph caused some tree limbs to fall and sporadic power outages did occur due to the ice and wind combination.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kansas Highway Patrol reported ice covered elevated surfaces with around one quarter inch of ice on flat surfaces such as cars and other elevated surfaces above the ground.  Other law enforcement across Riley county reported similar ice build up on elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow, sleet and mainly freezing rain fell across all of the area during the early morning hours of January 17th.  Most observation sites measured anywhere from two tenths to nearly four tenths of flat ice accumulation from this event.  Impacts did include some sporadic power outages and hazardous travel conditions.  However, air temperatures rose to above freezing by mid to late morning on January 17th mitigating more severe impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS measured 0.35 inches of flat ice accumulation at Emporia Airport.  Impacts were limited however as air temperatures rose to above freezing by late morning melting off much of the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level disturbance pushed across the CWA into the overnight hours on the 28th and throughout the day on the 29th.  The heaviest snow fell at the beginning of the event. Moist upslope flow ahead of this system allowed for some high accumulating snow, especially for southern counties stretching from Liberal to Bucklin pushing around the foot mark with areas north around 6 inches in the Dodge City area and less further north towards Hays. A winter storm warning was issued from Finney to Seward County and points east to Edwards and Comanche. All other areas were in winter weather advisories that saw less accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 9.0 inches was measured in Liberal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Windy conditions occurred behind an Arctic front on this Thursday evening. The day began with north central Kansas in the warm sector of an occluded 996 mb low pressure system over eastern Colorado. As the day progressed, the low filled to 1000 mb and moved into central Kansas. Meanwhile, an Arctic front accelerated southward and merged with the fronts associated with this low. The momentum resulted in a continued southward surge of the combined front, with its passage in north central Kansas occurring early in the evening. In its wake, north winds increased to around 30 mph, with frequent gusts around 45 mph. Higher one-time peak gusts occurred between 50 and 62 mph in some spots. The highest wind gusts were 62 mph at Beloit and 58 mph at Randall. However, these stations are unofficial and it is unknown if these they are properly sited. Peak gusts at official observation sites included 55 mph at the Beloit airport, 53 mph at Smith Center airport, and 52 mph at Phillipsburg airport. While windy conditions continued through the night, the highest gusts were recorded between 7 PM and midnight CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A post-frontal non-thunderstorm wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 1 mile south-southeast of Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved east then across the CO/KS state line and then began to move northeast across northwest Kansas. The line of storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 85 mph, up to penny size hail, and heavy rain up to 2.6 inches. The heavy rain caused localized Flash Flooding in parts of Sherman and Thomas counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KGLD in Goodland measured a wind gust of 61 mph that was sustained for 2 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight severe risk was draped across much of the CWA due to a surface convergent boundary over western counties with an approaching cold front from the north. Supercell thunderstorms developed along the convergent boundary during the late afternoon before forming into a complex line of storms along the front that lasted into the overnight hours. There was ample instability with CAPE over 2000J/kg coupled with decent shear up to 30KT and moisture influx due to the more saturated and unstable southeast low level flow. Early on with the supercells, funnel clouds and large hail were the main threats. Once the line developed and pushed south and east, damaging winds and hail became the hazards with many reports that verified a number of the warnings that were issued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight severe risk was draped across much of the CWA due to a surface convergent boundary over western counties with an approaching cold front from the north. Supercell thunderstorms developed along the convergent boundary during the late afternoon before forming into a complex line of storms along the front that lasted into the overnight hours. There was ample instability with CAPE over 2000J/kg coupled with decent shear up to 30KT and moisture influx due to the more saturated and unstable southeast low level flow. Early on with the supercells, funnel clouds and large hail were the main threats. Once the line developed and pushed south and east, damaging winds and hail became the hazards with many reports that verified a number of the warnings that were issued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three rounds of storms occurred as a front interacted with very unstable air to produce numerous severe thunderstorms that caused extensive hail and wind damage over portions of the area.||The second round of storms developed near Topeka, KS and roared southeastward into east-central Missouri in the early afternoon, producing substantial straight-line wind damage.  ||The final round of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and evening along a warm front over southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri, producing primarily large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous power poles and lines causing over 2000 power outages between Elsmore, Hiatville and Devon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning cluster of elevated supercell thunderstorms produced very large hail across portions of central Kansas on May 3rd, 2020. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The late evening of Monday the 24th into the morning of Tuesday the 25th featured one of the most intriguing snow events of the 2019-20 winter season within this six-county North Central Kansas area. While roughly the northeast half of the area (particularly Jewell/Mitchell counties) received little to no overnight accumulation, an intense/narrow mesoscale band dumped as much as 6-9 of wet snow along a northwest-southeast axis through the heart of Phillips and Osborne counties (and clipping northeast Rooks and southwest Smith counties along the way).  Amounts this high concentrated within a swath no more than 10-15 miles wide, with totals tapering off to barely a dusting only a few miles on either side. Per several official reports (NWS coop observer/CoCoRaHS) and a few unofficial ones, some of the highest totals featured: 9.2 six miles east of Phillipsburg, 8.1 in Phillipsburg, 8 at Logan and 7-9 sixteen miles south-southeast of Osborne. To the southwest of the main band, more modest amounts included 3.2 four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 2 at Natoma. Despite the hefty totals within the primary snow stripe, the fact that this was a late night/early morning event and that winds averaged only 10-15 MPH through most of it kept travel impacts somewhat in check through roughly sunrise. However, north winds picked up as the day of the 25th wore on, with frequent gusts of 25-35 MPH combining with residual light snowfall to promote some drifting and reduced visibility. ||Breaking down timing, the main event associated with the intense mesoscale band unfolded over primarily a 7-9 hour span, getting underway between 11 p.m. and 2 a.m. as spotty light rain transitioned to steady snow, and weakening/gradually sinking south of the area between 7-9 a.m. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was a potent shortwave trough diving into the Central Plains from the north-northwest, featuring a compact/intense closed circulation that peaked in intensity while traversing the local area (especially evident at 700 millibars). As the already-strong zone of deep convergence/frontogenesis intercepted a pocket of steep mid-level lapse rates, the pronounced mesoscale band blossomed, starting out oriented predominantly north-south but gradually pivoting considerably more northwest-southeast in response to the movement of the parent system. At the surface, the relatively light breezes through the night resulted from the region temporarily residing within the heart of an inverted trough axis, before this boundary slipped south around sunrise and allowed stronger northerly winds to kick in. Temperature-wise, the wet nature of snow resulted from overnight readings holding up mainly in the low 30s F. From a forecast perspective this was a challenging event, as although it became increasingly evident 24-48 hours out that mesoscale banding might ensue, its eventual placement/intensity remained a major question mark until it materialized on radar. In the end, although forecasted maximum snow amounts proved to be significantly under-done, a good chance for at least 2-4 within the broader general area became apparent by the afternoon of the 24th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Weather Advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports generally ranged from 6 to 9 inches, with the highest amounts falling in a narrow 10-15 mile wide corridor that ran north-northwest to southeast through the heart of the county. Extreme northeastern portions of the county had very little snow. NWS cooperative observer and trained spotter totals included 9.2 inches six miles east of Phillipsburg, 8.1 inches in Phillipsburg, 8 inches in Logan and 7 inches at Kirwin Dam. Public-reported totals included 5 inches seven miles north of Phillipsburg and 5 inches seven miles east-southeast of Prairie View.

As the already-strong zone of deep convergence/frontogenesis intercepted a pocket of steep mid-level lapse rates, the pronounced mesoscale band blossomed, starting out oriented predominantly north-south but gradually pivoting considerably more northwest-southeast in response to the movement of the parent system. At the surface, the relatively light breezes through the night resulted from the region temporarily residing within the heart of an inverted trough axis, before this boundary slipped south around sunrise and allowed stronger northerly winds to kick in. Temperature-wise, the wet nature of snow resulted from overnight readings holding up mainly in the low 30s F. From a forecast perspective this was a challenging event, as although it became increasingly evident 24-48 hours out that mesoscale banding might ensue, its eventual placement/intensity remained a major question mark until it materialized on radar. In the end, although forecasted maximum snow amounts proved to be significantly under-done, a good chance for at least 2-4 within the broader general area became apparent by the afternoon of the 24th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Weather Advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on ground-truth storm total snowfall reports from adjacent counties and visible satellite data, it is highly likely that far northeastern portions of the county were clipped by a 10-15 mile wide corridor of amounts at least 6-8 inches. Totals quickly tapered off south of that corridor, with the majority of the county receiving no more than around 3 inches, included a NWS Cooperative Observer four miles west-northwest of Plainville reporting 3.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe t-storms some supercells developed across parts of northeast Kansas on the evening of May 14th.  At least 4 small short lived tornadoes were documented by storm chasers.  No damage was reported with any of the tornadoes so all will be classified as EFU although photos suggest they were small and short-lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chasers reported a small tornado on the ground south of Council Grove.  Photos were also taken showing a narrow funnel extending near the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms began developing over central Kansas very early on 5/4. Initial supercells produced very large hail, up to baseball size. The threat transitioned to more of a strong wind concern later in the morning. Winds up to 80 mph were reported with one storm and damage occurred in several different counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail reported by trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado occurred in Sherman County, Kansas on May 23rd, 2020. The tornado occurred for an estimated 3 minutes according to radar signatures and spotter accounts. No damage was reported with this tornado. Storms quickly developed in the afternoon hours, but it wasn't until early evening when any type of severe weather was produced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief landspout tornado. Exact times and distance estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight severe risk was draped across much of the CWA due to a surface convergent boundary over western counties with an approaching cold front from the north. Supercell thunderstorms developed along the convergent boundary during the late afternoon before forming into a complex line of storms along the front that lasted into the overnight hours. There was ample instability with CAPE over 2000J/kg coupled with decent shear up to 30KT and moisture influx due to the more saturated and unstable southeast low level flow. Early on with the supercells, funnel clouds and large hail were the main threats. Once the line developed and pushed south and east, damaging winds and hail became the hazards with many reports that verified a number of the warnings that were issued.

With ample enough shear over 30kts and low level moisture ahead of it, a number of storms developed along and ahead the boundary giving way to large hail close to 3 inches in diameter. The storms out of eastern Colorado well behind the front came with mainly damaging winds and flooding hazards being more of cluster and lines that moved through the far western counties of the CWA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight severe risk was draped across much of the CWA due to a surface convergent boundary over western counties with an approaching cold front from the north. Supercell thunderstorms developed along the convergent boundary during the late afternoon before forming into a complex line of storms along the front that lasted into the overnight hours. There was ample instability with CAPE over 2000J/kg coupled with decent shear up to 30KT and moisture influx due to the more saturated and unstable southeast low level flow. Early on with the supercells, funnel clouds and large hail were the main threats. Once the line developed and pushed south and east, damaging winds and hail became the hazards with many reports that verified a number of the warnings that were issued.

A slight severe risk for much of the CWA due to a cold front pushing in from the northwest. With ample enough shear over 30kts and low level moisture ahead of it, a number of storms developed along and ahead the boundary giving way to large hail close to 3 inches in diameter. The storms out of eastern Colorado well behind the front came with mainly damaging winds and flooding hazards being more of cluster and lines that moved through the far western counties of the CWA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight severe risk was draped across much of the CWA due to a surface convergent boundary over western counties with an approaching cold front from the north. Supercell thunderstorms developed along the convergent boundary during the late afternoon before forming into a complex line of storms along the front that lasted into the overnight hours. There was ample instability with CAPE over 2000J/kg coupled with decent shear up to 30KT and moisture influx due to the more saturated and unstable southeast low level flow. Early on with the supercells, funnel clouds and large hail were the main threats. Once the line developed and pushed south and east, damaging winds and hail became the hazards with many reports that verified a number of the warnings that were issued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front|during the afternoon and evening as a dry line and cold front moved across the region and interacted with a very unstable airmass. The storms produced widespread straight-line wind damage around Columbus, Kansas before moving into Missouri. Rainfall accumulations over an inch were widespread across the area, with localized amounts over one and a half inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were reported down in McCune via photos on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through Chautauqua, Elk, Montgomery and Wilson counties. The most significant damage occurred to a National Weather Service CO-OP observers residence, where damage occurred to a couple of out buildings and some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage occurred at a farmstead just south of Hale. Several 80+ year old trees were either uprooted or snapped at the base. Two machine sheds were damaged as well. The spotter was unsure of the wind speed but thought it was at least 80 mph if not stronger.  The Chautauqua Emergency Manager performed a damage survey (including an aerial survey with a drone) and was able to determine that the damage was caused by straight line downburst winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of a little less than 1/2 of an inch 12 miles northwest of Coldwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved into Kansas bringing quite a bit of moisture with it.  The main issue with this system would be temperatures and the precipitation type.  Trying to decipher the liquid/frozen line would prove difficult.  Once the transition to ice did occur, it did not last long but did hit with a cause significant issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow then freezing rain occurred across the county with most locations between 0.1 and 0.25 inches of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level disturbance pushed across the CWA into the overnight hours on the 28th and throughout the day on the 29th.  The heaviest snow fell at the beginning of the event. Moist upslope flow ahead of this system allowed for some high accumulating snow, especially for southern counties stretching from Liberal to Bucklin pushing around the foot mark with areas north around 6 inches in the Dodge City area and less further north towards Hays. A winter storm warning was issued from Finney to Seward County and points east to Edwards and Comanche. All other areas were in winter weather advisories that saw less accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 13 inches was observed at a location 5 miles south of Sublette.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light freezing rain and dense freezing fog event occurred between the 16th and 17th of January in northwest Kansas. Ice accumulations and poor visibility led to dangerous driving conditions across the area, although only one car accident was reported in northwest Kansas and roads largely remained open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported a rollover accident caused by icy road conditions about 3 miles north of the Greeley County line along KS Hwy 27. Local deputies on the scene of the accident reported as much as half an inch of ice accumulation on the roadway and that it was very dangerous to walk on.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather producing system moved across the area bringing a mixture of inclement weather, some of which was convective in nature.  Much of precipitation began as liquid, however, the further north in the state, the transition to ice began.  With a strong surface front moving through, cold air was ushered in at all locations and the liquid and freezing precipitation soon turned to frozen precipitation in the form of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few instances of severe winds occurred across portions of north central Kansas just after sunrise on the morning of the 4th. Widespread rain and weak convection occurred the previous night across most of south central Nebraska, but the thunderstorms that produced the severe winds across Smith County were part of a much larger arc of thunderstorms that developed from central into eastern Kansas around and just after sunrise. The majority of this arc of thunderstorms grew upscale and organized just southeast of the coverage area and ultimately went on to produce fairly widespread severe hail and wind across central and eastern Kansas and adjacent portions of western Missouri during the mid to late morning hours of the 4th. There were only two severe wind reports, both in Smith County, and no reports of wind damage, suggesting that the majority of thunderstorms over the local area were sub-severe.||The thunderstorms developed along a zone of low level warm air advection and deep moisture convergence, associated with the nose of a veering 25-30 kt low level jet, co-located with a coupled upper jet structure noted on the 12Z 300mb upper air analysis chart. A moderately strong vorticity lobe ejecting east-northeast across the central Plains from a much larger upper trough situated over the northern Rockies also aided in lift. Thunderstorms were fueled by a large reservoir of most unstable CAPE values of 2000-3000 J/kg across central Kansas, fed north and east into the arc of thunderstorms by the aforementioned low level jet. Effective shear was also impressive, on the order of 45-55 kt, owing to south-southeasterly low level winds veering and increasing to over 50kt in the mid levels. As such, instability and shear was more than sufficient for severe thunderstorms, with the greatest combination noted just south and east of the local area.

A slight severe risk for much of the CWA due to a cold front pushing in from the northwest. With ample enough shear over 30kts and low level moisture ahead of it, a number of storms developed along and ahead the boundary giving way to large hail close to 3 inches in diameter. The storms out of eastern Colorado well behind the front came with mainly damaging winds and flooding hazards being more of cluster and lines that moved through the far western counties of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a brief landspout tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three rounds of storms occurred as a front interacted with very unstable air to produce numerous severe thunderstorms that caused extensive hail and wind damage over portions of the area.||The second round of storms developed near Topeka, KS and roared southeastward into east-central Missouri in the early afternoon, producing substantial straight-line wind damage.  ||The final round of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and evening along a warm front over southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri, producing primarily large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to two inches in size damaged several vehicles and caused minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although most all impacts from light wintry precipitation (mainly freezing drizzle and resultant power outages) focused slightly north of the state line in southern Nebraska, this was nonetheless a crummy Easter Sunday weather-wise in North Central Kansas. The entire area endured relentless north winds and impressively-cold temperatures by mid-April standards, with gusts commonly 40-50+ MPH and daytime highs only in the mid-upper 30s F (roughly 30 degrees below normal), promoting wind chill values only in the teens to low 20s F. While peak gusts at most local weather stations topped out slightly below severe-criteria, one personal weather station in far northeastern Jewell County clocked a 62 MPH gust during the afternoon. Fortunately, wintry precipitation was extremely-minimal across this area, consisting of no more than a dusting of snow and periods of (mostly non-freezing) drizzle. ||As for the meteorological background behind this unpleasant Easter, the main player in the mid-upper levels was a large-scale trough traversing the central CONUS, highlighted by broad low pressure and various embedded disturbances extending southward from southern Canada, and also a potent southern stream shortwave trough ejecting east-northeast across Texas. Given that North Central Kansas resided slightly south of the main northern stream disturbance, mid-level saturation lacked appreciably, keeping all but very light precipitation at bay. At the surface, a powerful cold front blasted through the local area during the pre-dawn hours of the 12th, turning the pleasant temperatures in the 70s to low 80s F on the 11th into a fleeting memory. The intense north winds were driven by a stout pressure gradient between a roughly 997 millibar low tracking east across the Southern Plains and a 1032 millibar high emanating southeastward from the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 MPH was recorded by a mesonet station located 6 miles east-northeast of Webber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong gradient strengthened across the area as an Arctic cold front made its way through the high plains. North winds behind the front lasted for several hours with peak gusts over 50 mph in some places with peaks around 60 mph for only a couple of locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the Garden City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper low led to narrow bands (one county wide) of heavy snow across portions of central Kansas on February 25th, 2020.  This heavy snow band had reports of 8 to 12 inches with additional snowfall amounts occurring further south as the band shifts to the southeast with 5 to 8 inches. Interstate 70 was closed between Salina and Ellsworth Kansas, for a brief period of time due to numerous accidents blocking the interstate. Numerous slide offs have also occurred along Interstate 135 south of Salina. Kansas High Patrol reported from 0400-1200 CST, there were calls of 42 slide offs, 7 property only collisions and 1 fatal collision.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow reports started coming in around 630AM with 5 inches measured in Sylvan Grove. As snow continued to fall, amounts rose near Sylvan Grove. In the early afternoon, a total of 13 inches was reported 2 E of Sylvan Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system lifted out of the Desert Southwest and across the southern Plains during the early morning hours of February 12th, 2020. Snow overspread southern Kansas a couple hours prior to sunrise and increased intensity as the morning commute approached. Temperatures near or slightly above freezing allowed for a heavy wet snow, with much of it melting by the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts across the county ranged between 1 and 4 inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure system moved across potions of central and south central Kansas during the late evening hours or June 9th and the early morning hours of June 10th, 2020. Tree limbs were knocked down across central and south central Kansas, with most of the limbs 5 to 6 inches in diameter.  Some power pole were also knocked down.  The winds gusted as high as 59 mph at Wichita Eisenhower National airport, with numerous reports of tree damage in Wichita, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the afternoon hours on February 24th and the evening of February 25th, a system brought snow and gusty winds to northwest Kansas. While snowfall accumulation was not significant, snow caused roadways to become slick throughout northwestern Kansas leading to several accidents. Several snow squall warnings were issued due to brief periods of blowing snow leading to reduced visibilties associated with a heavy band of snow moving from north to south across northwest Kansas and far east-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall conditions were presumed to have occurred based on proximity to other counties meeting criteria as well as radar signatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the afternoon hours on February 24th and the evening of February 25th, a system brought snow and gusty winds to northwest Kansas. While snowfall accumulation was not significant, snow caused roadways to become slick throughout northwestern Kansas leading to several accidents. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of the final few days of July a trough dug into the central part of the CONUS and produced several days of moderate to heavy rain. While the bulk of the flooding occurred on the Missouri side, there was some isolated flooding in Miami County Kansas. Some of the highest totals from the area ranged from 6 to 10 inches of rain. Despite the high rainfall amounts, only minor damage resulted from the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off and on moderate to heavy rain caused several areas to reach 6-10 inches of rainfall. One area that experienced some minor flash flooding was around the Hillsdale area, where a normally dry creek bed was running swiftly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through during the late afternoon and there were surface based thunderstorms near the Oklahoma border.  However, with a strong upper level shortwave there was strong lifting above the relative shallow cold air behind the front. Therefore, there were elevated thunderstorms above the cold air and some became severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold front brings slow moving thunderstorms and heavy rain to extreme southern portions of south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening of July 26. Thunderstorms developed between 3pm and 4pm CDT from southwest to northeast, generally along a line from Rooks County in north central Kansas, to Thayer County in south central Nebraska. Once thunderstorms developed, heavy rain quickly became the main concern, though a few non-severe wind gusts around 50 to 55 mph were reported across north central Kansas early on in the event. The heavy rain threat was driven by several factors, including: weak mid and upper level winds which resulted in slow storm motion, training of thunderstorms over the same areas for several hours, and unusually high atmospheric moisture levels. In fact, precipitable water values were near 2��� on this evening, which is near 3 standard deviations above normal and in the upper 90th percentile of climatology. Heavy rain rates of 1-2��� per hour, or higher, were common under the strongest thunderstorm cores, especially between the hours of 4pm and 9pm CDT. Thunderstorms gradually decreased in intensity during the late evening as the persistent convection over the same areas and loss of daytime heating led to decreasing instability. The majority of the lingering light rain ended by midnight.||Heavy rain occurred across most of north central Kansas, with the exception of northern Phillips County and northern Smith County. Storm total rain amounts by the morning of July 27th of 3-4.5��� inches were common, with an isolated maximum of 6-7��� reported in south central Osborne County. The highest reported rain amount was 6.72��� measured by an NWS Cooperative Observer located 9 miles north-northeast of Natoma, KS. The primary impact from the heavy rain was water affecting county roads. Just north of Randall, KS, the flooding contributed to a car losing control and ending up in the ditch, with the driver needing to be rescued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches across the area. The primary impact was water affecting county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving upper low brings multiple rounds of thunderstorms, including a few severe thunderstorms with high wind, to north central Kansas from the late afternoon hours of July 29th into the early morning hours of July 30th. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the mid to late morning hours, but this activity remained weak and heavy rain remained off to the north across south central Nebraska. New thunderstorms developed from central Nebraska into northwest Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours as instability increased. Activity grew upscale into thunderstorms clusters and moved east/southeast during the evening hours. One such cluster moved through Philips and Rooks Counties between 6-8pm CDT, but remained sub-severe. Additional thunderstorms developed and organized into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) west of the area during the late evening hours, then moved into Phillips and Rooks Counties around midnight. A portion of the line began to develop a bow between midnight and 1am which produced a swath of severe winds from Phillips and Rook Counties southeast into Osborne County. The strongest wind gust was 72mph as measured by a mesonet station 9 miles north-northeast of Codell. It is not known what, if any, damage occurred as a result of the severe winds. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts occurred with a line of thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska and north central Kansas on the evening of July 2nd. Fairly typical summertime weather pattern occurred on this day in which scattered thunderstorms developed over the High Plains during the late afternoon, posing a tornado and large hail threat, then organized into a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) as they shifted eastward through the evening into an increasing moist, and unstable environment. As is often the case with QLCS events in this part of the country during this time of year, severe wind gusts were the primary threat. Thunderstorm mergers over extreme southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas promoted the development of a significant pool of cold air which caused a segment of thunderstorms, within the broader line of thunderstorms, to quickly accelerate eastward into western portions of the County Warning Area (CWA) between 7-8pm CDT. By the time this activity arrived it already had a history of producing widespread 60-70mph wind gusts, and even isolated 70-80+mph wind gusts in the vicinity of Hill City, Kansas. The bowing line of thunderstorms continued to produce a swath of severe winds as it moved through the southern half (or so) of the CWA through the late evening hours. For north central Kansas, severe wind reports were most concentrated across areas south of Highway 36 and west of Highway 281. The strongest reported gust was 70mph in Plainville, Kansas. Damage to trees and power lines were the main impacts of the severe wind. The strongest thunderstorm cores, on the leading edge of the QLCS, exited the area to the southeast by around midnight. Trailing stratiform rain lingered for a couple more hours.||As mentioned above, the meteorological conditions were quite typical for this area for early July. A warm and moist air mass and steep mid level lapse rates supported very strong instability (MLCAPE 4000-5000 J/kg) across a large portion of the Central Plains. A broad upper level ridge axis was in place from the southern Plains into the Upper Mississippi River Valley, resulting in weak southwesterly flow aloft. Because of the upper ridging, deep layer wind shear was weak at less than 30 knots, with much of the shear owing to veering of wind direction in the low to mid levels. This indicates that primarily mesoscale features such as an MCV and a strong cold pool were the main factors that supported severe wind in this event, and not synoptic scale features. Downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg were indicative of the strong potential for cool pool development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on 30 Road between E and F Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive line of thunderstorms brought widespread severe wind to much of south central Nebraska and portions of north central Kansas on this date. This event will likely go down as the most widespread severe weather event of the spring and summer of 2020 for the Hastings County Warning Area (CWA). Thunderstorms developed along a cold front across north central Nebraska, and also within a zone of low level upslope flow over western Nebraska and northeast Colorado, during the early evening hours of July 8th. Both areas of development initially contained line segments and embedded supercells, posing risks for all severe hazards, but quickly organized into two separate lines of convection, or quasi linear convective systems (QLCSs). By 9pm CDT, one line extended roughly west to east from Thedford, NE to O'Neill, NE and was moving southeast, while the other line extended roughly north to south from near Thedford, NE to just west of McCook, NE and was moving mainly east. Shortly thereafter, both lines began to merge together into one coherent line and produced several instances of 60-75 mph wind gusts and isolated severe hail north/northwest of the CWA. Thunderstorms increased in organization into a bow echo over Furnas and Gosper Counties in Nebraska, then entered far western/northwestern portions of north central Kansas between 10:30-11pm CDT. By 11pm CDT, the QLCS had taken on an ���S��� shape, slanting southwest to northeast. The greatest concentration of severe wind reports occurred north of the state line, however, several severe wind reports were also reported roughly along and north of Highway 36 between 11pm CDT on July 8th and 1am CDT on July 9th as forward speed increased dramatically. Peak wind gusts across north central Kansas generally remained 60-65mph. The only known damage was to a roof of a mobile home in Mitchell County, though at least minor tree and crop damage likely occurred across at least portions of the area as well. The leading edge of the QLCS, and associated peak wind threat, shifted east and southeast of the CWA by 1AM CDT, though lingering stratiform rain as well as a northwest to southeast band of thunderstorms near the state line continued for a few more hours. Severe hail associated with this activity remained just north of the state line, over Furnas County, where regenerating thunderstorms were most intense.||Meteorologically speaking, this severe wind event was driven largely by a cold front interacting with a strongly unstable airmass due to steep mid level lapse rates, strong heating, and moist boundary layer. Initially cellular activity quickly grew upscale into line segments owing to the linear forcing along the cold front and generally outflow dominated activity, with peak DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg. The primary shortwave trough responsible for the cold front remained well north of the area, near the Montana/Canadian border, but several weak perturbations moving west to east over the cold front likely aided in thunderstorm development as well. A distinct mesoscale convective vortex, modest deep layer shear of 30-35 knots, and an intensifying southerly low level jet up to 45 knots sustained the severe convection well past sunset. Finally, it's worth noting that an impressive surface pressure rise/fall couplet of at least 3mb/2hr accompanied the QLCS across the CWA between 05Z and 07Z.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 65 MPH caused roof damage to a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind gusts to portions of north central Kansas the evening of July 18th. Thunderstorms initially developed across northwest Kansas, just west of WFO Hastings CWA, between 5pm and 6pm CDT, along and ahead of a southeastward moving cold front. Conditions ahead of the front were very hot and humid, and a Heat Advisory was in effect for much of the daytime hours on the 18th. A few small clusters of thunderstorms within a broader corridor of convection ahead of the front became more organized between 6pm to 7pm CDT, with one such cluster becoming severe between Hoxie and Hill City. A wind gust of 72mph was reported just west of the area in Graham County just before 8pm CDT. This activity entered Rooks County around 8pm CDT, bringing damaging wind gusts near 60mph. This caused damage to a couple of campers at Webster State Park. This was the only known damage report from this activity within the Hastings CWA and thunderstorms decreased in intensity after 9pm due to stabilizing boundary layer and weak wind shear which allowed outflow to outrun the main convection.||The meteorological setup was ripe for localized damaging winds owing to strong instability (MLCAPE 3000-4000 J/kg), weak deep layer wind shear (effective shear 20-25kt), and a deeply mixed boundary layer resulting in downdraft CAPE (DCAPE) of 1300-1500 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH. Lawn furniture was blown around 6 miles west-northwest of Webster State Park, while some minor camper damage was reported at Webster State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold front brings slow moving thunderstorms and heavy rain to extreme southern portions of south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening of July 26. Thunderstorms developed between 3pm and 4pm CDT from southwest to northeast, generally along a line from Rooks County in north central Kansas, to Thayer County in south central Nebraska. Once thunderstorms developed, heavy rain quickly became the main concern, though a few non-severe wind gusts around 50 to 55 mph were reported across north central Kansas early on in the event. The heavy rain threat was driven by several factors, including: weak mid and upper level winds which resulted in slow storm motion, training of thunderstorms over the same areas for several hours, and unusually high atmospheric moisture levels. In fact, precipitable water values were near 2��� on this evening, which is near 3 standard deviations above normal and in the upper 90th percentile of climatology. Heavy rain rates of 1-2��� per hour, or higher, were common under the strongest thunderstorm cores, especially between the hours of 4pm and 9pm CDT. Thunderstorms gradually decreased in intensity during the late evening as the persistent convection over the same areas and loss of daytime heating led to decreasing instability. The majority of the lingering light rain ended by midnight.||Heavy rain occurred across most of north central Kansas, with the exception of northern Phillips County and northern Smith County. Storm total rain amounts by the morning of July 27th of 3-4.5��� inches were common, with an isolated maximum of 6-7��� reported in south central Osborne County. The highest reported rain amount was 6.72��� measured by an NWS Cooperative Observer located 9 miles north-northeast of Natoma, KS. The primary impact from the heavy rain was water affecting county roads. Just north of Randall, KS, the flooding contributed to a car losing control and ending up in the ditch, with the driver needing to be rescued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches across the area. Law enforcement reported that multiple roads were closed to running water. Just north of Randall, a car left the road and ended up in the ditch, with the driver needing rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very humid air and above normal temperatures combined to produce extreme instability across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of July 11, 2020. Scattered thunderstorms developed across east central Kansas during the afternoon hours and spread slowly south and east across southeast Kansas through the late afternoon and early evening hours. Some of the storms produced very large hail around the size of softballs as well as numerous reports of damaging winds. The Oswego area experienced more widespread wind damage with estimated speeds around 90 mph with isolated higher gusts.

The greatest concentration of severe wind reports occurred north of the state line, however, several severe wind reports were also reported roughly along and north of Highway 36 between 11pm CDT on July 8th and 1am CDT on July 9th as forward speed increased dramatically. Peak wind gusts across north central Kansas generally remained 60-65mph. The only known damage was to a roof of a mobile home in Mitchell County, though at least minor tree and crop damage likely occurred across at least portions of the area as well. The leading edge of the QLCS, and associated peak wind threat, shifted east and southeast of the CWA by 1AM CDT, though lingering stratiform rain as well as a northwest to southeast band of thunderstorms near the state line continued for a few more hours. Severe hail associated with this activity remained just north of the state line, over Furnas County, where regenerating thunderstorms were most intense.||Meteorologically speaking, this severe wind event was driven largely by a cold front interacting with a strongly unstable airmass due to steep mid level lapse rates, strong heating, and moist boundary layer. Initially cellular activity quickly grew upscale into line segments owing to the linear forcing along the cold front and generally outflow dominated activity, with peak DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg. The primary shortwave trough responsible for the cold front remained well north of the area, near the Montana/Canadian border, but several weak perturbations moving west to east over the cold front likely aided in thunderstorm development as well. A distinct mesoscale convective vortex, modest deep layer shear of 30-35 knots, and an intensifying southerly low level jet up to 45 knots sustained the severe convection well past sunset. Finally, it's worth noting that an impressive surface pressure rise/fall couplet of at least 3mb/2hr accompanied the QLCS across the CWA between 05Z and 07Z.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 MPH was recorded by a mesonet station located in Randall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near normal to below normal precipitation amounts occurred for Northwest Kansas during November.  Precipitation amounts ranged from 0-0.50.  Normal precipitation amounts for November range from 0.25-1.  The D2 Severe Drought level expanded slightly further east.  However the D3 Extreme Drought expanded east to include most of Northwest Kansas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0.01-0.50, which was half an inch to an inch below normal.  The precipitation amounts declined to the southeast.  Normal precipitation amounts are 0.50-1. Key impacts: vegetation more favorable for rapid fire growth, little to no soil moisture, the ground is more favorable for blowing dust.

Severe wind gusts occurred with a line of thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska and north central Kansas on the evening of July 2nd. Fairly typical summertime weather pattern occurred on this day in which scattered thunderstorms developed over the High Plains during the late afternoon, posing a tornado and large hail threat, then organized into a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) as they shifted eastward through the evening into an increasing moist, and unstable environment. As is often the case with QLCS events in this part of the country during this time of year, severe wind gusts were the primary threat. Thunderstorm mergers over extreme southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas promoted the development of a significant pool of cold air which caused a segment of thunderstorms, within the broader line of thunderstorms, to quickly accelerate eastward into western portions of the County Warning Area (CWA) between 7-8pm CDT. By the time this activity arrived it already had a history of producing widespread 60-70mph wind gusts, and even isolated 70-80+mph wind gusts in the vicinity of Hill City, Kansas. The bowing line of thunderstorms continued to produce a swath of severe winds as it moved through the southern half (or so) of the CWA through the late evening hours. For north central Kansas, severe wind reports were most concentrated across areas south of Highway 36 and west of Highway 281. The strongest reported gust was 70mph in Plainville, Kansas. Damage to trees and power lines were the main impacts of the severe wind. The strongest thunderstorm cores, on the leading edge of the QLCS, exited the area to the southeast by around midnight. Trailing stratiform rain lingered for a couple more hours.||As mentioned above, the meteorological conditions were quite typical for this area for early July. A warm and moist air mass and steep mid level lapse rates supported very strong instability (MLCAPE 4000-5000 J/kg) across a large portion of the Central Plains. A broad upper level ridge axis was in place from the southern Plains into the Upper Mississippi River Valley, resulting in weak southwesterly flow aloft. Because of the upper ridging, deep layer wind shear was weak at less than 30 knots, with much of the shear owing to veering of wind direction in the low to mid levels. This indicates that primarily mesoscale features such as an MCV and a strong cold pool were the main factors that supported severe wind in this event, and not synoptic scale features. Downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg were indicative of the strong potential for cool pool development.

By the time this activity arrived it already had a history of producing widespread 60-70mph wind gusts, and even isolated 70-80+mph wind gusts in the vicinity of Hill City, Kansas. The bowing line of thunderstorms continued to produce a swath of severe winds as it moved through the southern half (or so) of the CWA through the late evening hours. For north central Kansas, severe wind reports were most concentrated across areas south of Highway 36 and west of Highway 281. The strongest reported gust was 70mph in Plainville, Kansas. Damage to trees and power lines were the main impacts of the severe wind. The strongest thunderstorm cores, on the leading edge of the QLCS, exited the area to the southeast by around midnight. Trailing stratiform rain lingered for a couple more hours.||As mentioned above, the meteorological conditions were quite typical for this area for early July. A warm and moist air mass and steep mid level lapse rates supported very strong instability (MLCAPE 4000-5000 J/kg) across a large portion of the Central Plains. A broad upper level ridge axis was in place from the southern Plains into the Upper Mississippi River Valley, resulting in weak southwesterly flow aloft. Because of the upper ridging, deep layer wind shear was weak at less than 30 knots, with much of the shear owing to veering of wind direction in the low to mid levels. This indicates that primarily mesoscale features such as an MCV and a strong cold pool were the main factors that supported severe wind in this event, and not synoptic scale features. Downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg were indicative of the strong potential for cool pool development.

A 52 mph wind gust occurred near Canton and a 51 mph gust was reported near New Cambria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official NWS measurement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very humid air and above normal temperatures combined to produce extreme instability across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of July 11, 2020. Scattered thunderstorms developed across east central Kansas during the afternoon hours and spread slowly south and east across southeast Kansas through the late afternoon and early evening hours. Some of the storms produced very large hail around the size of softballs as well as numerous reports of damaging winds. The Oswego area experienced more widespread wind damage with estimated speeds around 90 mph with isolated higher gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The picture was sent over from KAKE TV.  The image showed the hail being just slightly smaller than a baseball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred across areas of Northwest Kansas during the morning of October 25th followed by snow during the evening into the afternoon of October 26th. This led to vehicle accidents during this time.  Up to six inches of snow was reported, with the highest snow reported in Sherman County.  Ice accumulations of up to 3/8 were reported, with the highest amounts in Wallace County on tree branches.  Accidents were reported in Greely, Wichita, Sherman, Cheyenne, Norton, and Thomas counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi-truck was reported to jack-knife at US-283 & Rd. B near the NE/KS state line at 8:19 MST on Oct 26th due to hazardous driving conditions because of freezing rain and snow. No injuries were reported. A report of 4 inches of total snowfall in Norton, KS was received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred across areas of Northwest Kansas during the morning of October 25th followed by snow during the evening into the afternoon of October 26th. This led to vehicle accidents during this time.  Up to six inches of snow was reported, with the highest snow reported in Sherman County.  Ice accumulations of up to 3/8 were reported, with the highest amounts in Wallace County on tree branches.  Accidents were reported in Greely, Wichita, Sherman, Cheyenne, Norton, and Thomas counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fall reports of 6-7 inches occurred in Yuma County, far southern Dundy, and central Sherman County. Based on proximity these amounts should have also occurred in Cheyenne County. A vehicle rollover due to hazardous driving conditions caused by freezing rain and snow was reported at the intersection of US-36 and Rd. 33 at 13:46 CST on Oct. 25th. No injuries were reported in this accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high wind gusts ranging from 27 to 81 mph. These high winds lasted for various durations of time, ranging from six minutes to one hour and fifty minutes after frontal passage. The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. The strong winds also caused a manure fire in southern Logan County to get out of hand; however, it was contained without causing damage. The most notable fire caused with this event was a grass fire caused from the embers of a burning trash fire in Graham County, which caused town-wide evacuations of Nicodemus. No injuries or structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across Logan County producing high winds with gusts ranging from 55 to 58 mph. The peak 58 mph gust was observed via the AWOS station near Oakley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of November 2020, and for the first time since 2015, a small portion of this six-county North Central Kansas area found itself in D2 Severe Drought per the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). Specifically, D2 was introduced to northern portions of Phillips and Smith counties along with the extreme northwest corner of Jewell County on the Nov. 3rd USDM issuance, with a slight southward expansion later in the month including a bit more of these counties. In addition, the Nov. 24th USDM technically introduced D3 Extreme Drought to the far northwest corner of Phillips County, marking the first instance of D3 within North Central Kansas since 2014. Outside of the fairly limited D2/D3 areas, the remainder of (and the majority of) the six-county area spent November in D1 Moderate Drought. ||Within a larger context, this autumn introduction of D2/D3 signaled a relatively-rapid decline in moisture conditions within North Central Kansas, as the entire six-county area had been void of all drought categories (including D0 Abnormally Dry) as recently as most of July and all of August (before D0/D1 started creeping in during September-October). In fact, July was notably wet across the vast majority of the area as most places tallied 5-10+ inches of rain (except for northwestern Phillips County which saw a bit less). But starting in August and peaking in October, the proverbial faucet really shut off, allowing drought to enter the picture. In fact, most of the area received no more than 5-10 percent-of-normal precipitation in October (including merely 0.05 at Smith Center and 0.09 at Phillipsburg). From a longer-duration perspective covering the four-month-stretch from August-November, a few of the very-driest NWS observer precipitation totals within/near the current D2/D3 area included 1.83 at Logan (driest on record), 1.88 at Phillipsburg (driest on record) and 2.59 in Smith Center (3rd-driest on record). Farther south and east within North Central Kansas, drought classification remained no worse than D1 during November, as much of especially Jewell, Osborne and Mitchell counties received slightly above normal monthly precipitation of 1.50-2.00+ inches. Given that the current North Central Kansas drought situation materialized fairly rapidly during the late summer/fall months and that the preceding few years had been rather wet, there were no known widespread/serious 2020 drought impacts within the local area through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of thunderstorms formed along boundaries in eastern Colorado and then merged into a cluster in Yuma County, Colorado. The cluster entered into northwest Kansas in the early evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts up to 60 MPH in Cheyenne and Rawlins County. The storms also produced up to penny sized hail in Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported sporadic dime to penny sized hail that fell for a duration of 20 minutes.

The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high wind gusts ranging from 27 to 81 mph. These high winds lasted for various durations of time, ranging from six minutes to one hour and fifty minutes after frontal passage. The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. The strong winds also caused a manure fire in southern Logan County to get out of hand; however, it was contained without causing damage. The most notable fire caused with this event was a grass fire caused from the embers of a burning trash fire in Graham County, which caused town-wide evacuations of Nicodemus. No injuries or structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across Wichita County producing 62 wind gusts that  lifted dirt into the air. Blowing dust reduced visibility to less than one-quarter mile in the town of Leoti for a duration of 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong northwesterly wind gusts during the late afternoon on November 14th. A strong low pressure system across southern Canada forced a cold front through north central Kansas during the late afternoon hours. Strong northwest wind gusts accompanied the passage of the front, particularly across the western third of the County Warning Area (CWA). A peak wind gust of 63 mph was measured in Phillips County, KS, but there was no known damage or impacts from the high wind. The severe wind gust was supported by intense pressure rises on the order of 4-5mb per 2 hours. The arrival time of the front during or just after peak mixing was also a contributing factor. Peak wind gusts further east were weaker, but still strong, at speeds of 50 to 55 mph, as far east as Highway 281. Peak wind gusts remained less than 50mph east of Highway 281 as the 2hr pressure rises decreased to 2-3 mb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 1 mile west of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high wind gusts ranging from 27 to 81 mph. These high winds lasted for various durations of time, ranging from six minutes to one hour and fifty minutes after frontal passage. The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. The strong winds also caused a manure fire in southern Logan County to get out of hand; however, it was contained without causing damage. The most notable fire caused with this event was a grass fire caused from the embers of a burning trash fire in Graham County, which caused town-wide evacuations of Nicodemus. No injuries or structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved slowly southeast across Graham County producing wind gusts ranging from 58 to 81 mph. The high winds lifted dust high into the air, which caused reduced visibilities as low as two hundred to one hundred yards in Hill City. These reduced visibilities caused one accident on Highway 283. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month the drought status for Greeley and Wallace counties remained unchanged.  The south half of Wallace County was in D2 Severe Drought.  Greeley County had D2 Severe Drought in the east half, with D3 Extreme Drought over the west half of the county.  Monthly precipitation ranged from 0.5 to 1 for both counties, which is up to 1 below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from 0.5 to 1.  This was up to 0.5 below normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of the 9th, light snow developed over Northwest Kansas. Snowfall amounts were less than an inch across Cheyenne and Sherman counties.  The snow changed to rain during the morning before ending. This snow event broke the record for the earliest snowfall of the season with the prior records being the 20th or 21st of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on snowfall reports from surrounding counties, believe that snowfall occurred in Cheyenne County even though there are no reports of snow.  Any snow reports would be less than an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near normal to below normal precipitation amounts occurred for Northwest Kansas during November. Precipitation amounts ranged from 0-0.50. Normal precipitation amounts for November range from 0.50-1. The D2 Severe Drought level expanded slightly further east. However the D3 Extreme Drought expanded east to include most of Northwest Kansas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0-0.10, which was 0.5-1 below normal. Normal precipitation amounts are 0.50-1. Key impacts: vegetation more favorable for rapid fire growth, little to no soil moisture, the ground is more favorable for blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0-0.25, which was 0.5-1 below normal. Normal precipitation amounts are 0.50-1.   The continued below normal precipitation caused D2 Severe Drought level to occur for Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county entered D2 Severe Drought.  Key impacts: vegetation more favorable for rapid fire growth, little to no soil moisture, the ground is more favorable for blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high atmospheric moisture, and a nearly stationary south    |to north orientated boundary, and southwest winds, generated several rounds of    |showers/embedded storms on September 8th, 2020.  The heavy rainfall produced some flooding across southeast Kansas with 2 to 5 inches of rainfall reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 24-hour rainfall amount reported was 4.5 inches. This report was received via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that developed into a line as it moved through northwest Kansas. The storms produced up to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts near 70 mph that produced brown out conditions and snapped numerous tree limbs in Logan, Thomas, Gove, and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported patches of blowing dust with brown out conditions just north of Oakley due to the leading edge of an outflow boundary producing strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved south into Northwest Kansas during the evening.  Wind gusts up to 60 MPH were recorded as the storms moved through, with the strongest gust occurring near St. Francis.  Hail up to two inches in diameter fell with these storms, with the largest hail reported near Herndon.  As the storms moved south their intensity declined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated the wind gusts to be 50-60 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of 5 power poles broken on the NW side of town due to thunderstorm wind gusts. Time is radar estimated and estimated wind gust speed is based on storm damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms formed over the Colorado High Plains increasing in coverage to form a line and intensified as they moved east into Kansas and Nebraska. In Kansas, the storms produced up to ping pong ball sized hail, up to 93 mph wind gusts, and up to 1.5 inches of rain. During the event, several power poles were reported down in Cheyenne, Logan, Thomas, and Graham counties. Five oil tank batteries in Graham County and a central pivot irrigation system in Thomas County were also damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KGLD measured a 61 mph wind gust from the core of a thunderstorm passing over the site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds affected portions of north central Kansas during the late morning and early afternoon hours on Sunday, November 8th. Peak wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were observed by airport AWOS stations as well as various mesonet stations between roughly 9am and 2pm CST. The primary areas affected were east of Highway 281. No significant damage was reported as a result of these strong winds.||The strong southerly winds occurred within a broad warm sector of an intense low pressure system tracking from the northern Rockies into the northern Plains. An anomalously strong low level jet developed over the Central Plains during the night of November 7th into the early morning hours of November 8th, in response to an increasing pressure gradient, and peaked at a magnitude of 60 to 70kt. Boundary layer mixing depth increased after sunrise on the morning of November 8th and managed to tap into the bottom of this low level jet, with the result being several hours of severe southerly synoptic winds being mixed down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH occurred during the late morning through early afternoon hours. A wind gust of 64 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located near Cawker City at 12:43 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell in an environment full of moisture and wind shear produced hail up to golf ball sized in Wichita County near Leoti.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of stationary thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall over Dundy County Nebraska expanded south into Cheyenne County.  Rainfall amounts of over eight inches fell, with the highest amounts of rain occurring near the Nebraska border.  Multiple roads had sections washed out, some as deep as 10 ft or so.  Some farms in the northern part of the county were stranded due to all the roads leading out being washed out.  Water in the Republican River was running so fast and deep that trees were snapping.  One bridge in the county was damaged and another was washed out from the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the road damage from flooding occurred north of CR S, with the damage becoming more severe toward the Nebraska border.  One bridge was partially damaged east of Highway 27 near CR Z.  Another bridge was washed out at River Road and RS 115.  A culvert was washed out on CR 19 between CR AA and CR BB.  Near CR BB and CR 17 a barb wire fence was washed onto the road.  Near CR 15 and CR V flood waters had washed a large tree onto the road.  Near CR 2 the Republican River was out of its banks.  Hackberry Creek was also flooding where it joined with the Republican River.  Damage from the flooding is unknown due to the unknown number of sections of road washed out and the unknown acres of crop land flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high wind gusts ranging from 27 to 81 mph. These high winds lasted for various durations of time, ranging from six minutes to one hour and fifty minutes after frontal passage. The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. The strong winds also caused a manure fire in southern Logan County to get out of hand; however, it was contained without causing damage. The most notable fire caused with this event was a grass fire caused from the embers of a burning trash fire in Graham County, which caused town-wide evacuations of Nicodemus. No injuries or structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across Norton County producing high winds. At the time of frontal passage, many mesonet stations and the AWOS have inaccurate or missing observations. The first observation reported by the Norton AWOS was at 7:55 pm CDT with a gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arguably the most noteworthy individual thunderstorm of the 2020 convective season within this six-county North Central Kansas area rolled southward across portions of Jewell, Mitchell and mainly eastern Osborne counties on this Friday evening, highlighted by several reports of large to very large hail. Of the counties impacted by this supercell, Jewell was hardest-hit, as copious amounts of tennis ball to softball size hail stones crashed down onto areas in and near the communities of Mankato and Jewell, causing extensive damage to homes, vehicles and area crops. The hail size toned down somewhat farther south into Mitchell and Osborne counties, where limited reports included ping pong ball size stones in Cawker City and golf ball size south of Osborne. Although damaging winds were not a major player during this event, several mesonets clocked gusts of at least 40-50 MPH, and a Kansas Mesonet site near Jewell reached severe-criteria with 65 MPH. Flooding was not much of an issue, with convection staying progressive enough to keep rainfall amounts largely under one inch.||Breaking down event timing and meteorological background, this giant storm invaded northern Jewell County between 7-8 pm. CDT, having already propagated south-southwestward for roughly 90 miles since its inception in east central Nebraska about three hours prior. As it drifted southward into Mitchell County between 8-9 p.m. CDT it started merging with a much smaller, separate storm that had flared up over central Osborne County. Finally, between 9-10 p.m. CDT, the now-merged supercell cluster continued southward while exiting the local area into central Kansas, ending all local storms for the night. This was actually a fairly conditional severe weather setup for North Central Kansas, with various pros and cons for/against robust activity. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided a fair distance south of a vigorous shortwave trough swinging across the Dakotas towards northern MN, resulting in fairly limited forcing aloft. Also working against convection (especially widespread severe) was a plume of warm mid level temperatures, with 700 millibar values of 12-14C promoting a healthy cap. On the other hand, low-level forcing was seasonably-strong, as a well-defined cold front slid southeastward into the area during the evening. Ahead of this boundary, a significantly-unstable airmass awaited, featuring temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F/dewpoints upper 60s-mid 70s F, yielding impressive mixed-layer CAPE up to around 4500 J/kg. This extreme instability, in the presence of sufficient deep-layer wind shear around 35 knots, clearly fostered a mesoscale environment capable of stout convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured by a Kansas Mesonet site located 3 miles west-northwest of Jewell.

CDT, having already propagated south-southwestward for roughly 90 miles since its inception in east central Nebraska about three hours prior. As it drifted southward into Mitchell County between 8-9 p.m. CDT it started merging with a much smaller, separate storm that had flared up over central Osborne County. Finally, between 9-10 p.m. CDT, the now-merged supercell cluster continued southward while exiting the local area into central Kansas, ending all local storms for the night. This was actually a fairly conditional severe weather setup for North Central Kansas, with various pros and cons for/against robust activity. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided a fair distance south of a vigorous shortwave trough swinging across the Dakotas towards northern MN, resulting in fairly limited forcing aloft. Also working against convection (especially widespread severe) was a plume of warm mid level temperatures, with 700 millibar values of 12-14C promoting a healthy cap. On the other hand, low-level forcing was seasonably-strong, as a well-defined cold front slid southeastward into the area during the evening. Ahead of this boundary, a significantly-unstable airmass awaited, featuring temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F/dewpoints upper 60s-mid 70s F, yielding impressive mixed-layer CAPE up to around 4500 J/kg. This extreme instability, in the presence of sufficient deep-layer wind shear around 35 knots, clearly fostered a mesoscale environment capable of stout convection.

A strong southeastward cold front produced high wind gusts ranging from 27 to 81 mph. These high winds lasted for various durations of time, ranging from six minutes to one hour and fifty minutes after frontal passage. The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. The strong winds also caused a manure fire in southern Logan County to get out of hand; however, it was contained without causing damage. The most notable fire caused with this event was a grass fire caused from the embers of a burning trash fire in Graham County, which caused town-wide evacuations of Nicodemus. No injuries or structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across Rawlins County producing strong to high wind gusts ranging from 40 mph to 64 mph. The high winds lifted dirt into the air, which reduced visibility to less than four blocks in the town of Atwood.
