EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across Graham County producing high winds sustained at 40 to 69 mph with gusts ranging from 58 to 81 mph. The Hill City ASOS station recorded a 66 mph peak gust. A mesonet station northeast of Bogue recorded a peak gust of 81 mph. During the event, Graham County Dispatch reported the high winds downed power lines which sparked a few sporadic fires around the county and cut power to the ASOS station after the 7:40 pm CDT observation. A more notable fire on south and southeast of Nicodemus due to the embers from a trash burning fire catching the grass on fire. No injuries or structural damage was reported from the fires. The high winds also lifted dirt into the air which caused reduced visibilities in Hill City of one hundred to two hundred yards. At 7:21 pm CDT, a car accident occurred due south of Hill City on Highway 283 due to the reduced visibilities. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell produced a substantial swath of very large hail in the Mankato and Jewell areas, with large hail produced for a significant amount of time. A public report of golf ball size hail was received at 7:45 p.m. CDT in Mankato. By 7:50 p.m. CDT, the hail size in Mankato reached a measured diameter of 4 inches, with tennis ball size hail still falling in town at 8:10 p.m. CDT. At 8:13 p.m. CDT, golf ball size hail was reported about 3 miles northwest of Randall. At 8:15 p.m. CDT, 4 inch diameter hail was reported 2 miles north of Jewell, with baseball size hail in Jewell. Significant hail damage to homes, vehicles and crops occurred in the area. As just one example, local media reported that the Jewell County EMS Director's vehicle incurred major hail damage, including a shattered windshield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low tracked across the Texas panhandle into northwestern Oklahoma that produced snow on the backside across southern counties of the CWA along the border. Reports up to a foot in the warning areas of Meade to Barber County with lesser amounts for the next tier of counties west and north of this line. Coupled with strong north winds gusting up to 30-40 mph, substantial impacts in the warning and advisory areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was observed in Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low again tracked across the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles, allowing for several inches of snow across much of the southern half of the CWA with highest reports near the Oklahoma state line. Broad banding of snow with the 700mb trough stretched across Seward to Clark Counties to include Meade, where the highest amounts fell up to 12-14 inches. Couple with the weak upslope easterly flow near the surface, this helped to aid in the widespread snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 9 inches was observed at a location 12 miles NW of Englewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For several hours on this late Sunday afternoon and evening, a handful of strong to severe storms advanced southward across portions of this six-county North Central Kansas area, yielding a few reports of marginally-severe hail up to quarter size mainly in portions of Jewell, Smith and Osborne counties. There were no significant ground-truth reports of thunderstorm winds, and all gusts captured by reliable area sensors came in under 50 MPH. ||This event consisted of isolated to scattered, hit-or-miss convection, as afternoon heating and subtle convergence near a weak cold front sagging southward across southern Nebraska prompted the development of several multicell storms/clusters. Locally, the first strong storm of the afternoon flared up between 3-4 p.m. CDT in southern Rooks County but fizzled quickly (no known reports). Then, between 530-630 p.m. CDT, a verified large-hailer over extreme southern Nebraska nearly brushed into northern Phillips County but weakened before doing so. Between 7-9 p.m. CDT strong to marginally-severe storms roamed mainly parts of Rooks/Smith/Jewell counties (hail reports from the Lebanon and Esbon areas). After this, it briefly appeared that the severe storm threat was over for the night, but around 10 p.m. CDT one last gasp of vigorous convection bubbled up near the Osborne/Mitchell County line, producing nickel to quarter size hail between Downs and Tipton before weakening. All activity departed south of the local area by 1130 p.m. CDT. In the end, the majority of regional severe weather concentrated at least slightly west, east and especially north of the local area. Nonetheless, this was a fairly potent setup for healthy storms in North Central Kansas, as afternoon surface dewpoints climbed into the mid 60s-low 70 F during the afternoon, promoting significant mixed-layer CAPE of at least 2000-4000 J/kg. In tandem with deep layer wind shear around 35 knots, this CAPE/shear combo proved plenty sufficient for robust convection. In the mid-upper levels, a pronounced north-northwest flow regime resided over the Central Plains, within the interface between a stout western United States ridge and a broad eastern trough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed in the early morning of December 29th and quickly become primarily freezing rain.  Ice accumulations of one quarter inch or more were reported across the northern portions of northeast Kansas mainly north of Interstate 70 where air temperatures stayed just below freezing for much of the day. Impacts however were muted due to the air temperatures gradually rising above freezing through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported of around one quarter inch of ice on elevated surfaces in Seneca, Kansas.  No other impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed in the early morning of December 29th and quickly become primarily freezing rain.  Ice accumulations of one quarter inch or more were reported across the northern portions of northeast Kansas mainly north of Interstate 70 where air temperatures stayed just below freezing for much of the day. Impacts however were muted due to the air temperatures gradually rising above freezing through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brown County DOT reported ice build up on elevated surfaces of more than one quarter inch to around .40 in Hiawatha.  A few sporadic power outages were also noted via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high wind gusts ranging from 27 to 81 mph. These high winds lasted for various durations of time, ranging from six minutes to one hour and fifty minutes after frontal passage. The high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to between one mile to as low as 10 yards at times across northwest Kansas. The strong winds knocked a few power lines down in Graham County which sparked a few small fires around the county and were quickly put out. The strong winds also caused a manure fire in southern Logan County to get out of hand; however, it was contained without causing damage. The most notable fire caused with this event was a grass fire caused from the embers of a burning trash fire in Graham County, which caused town-wide evacuations of Nicodemus. No injuries or structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved southeast across Sherman County producing winds with gusts between 55-80 mph. In northern Sherman County, up to 70 mph wind gusts were observed for a duration of one hour and 50 minutes. These strong winds snapped an 8 inch diameter tree limb off a fruit tree and another tree to fall, which crushed a fence, in Goodland. The Goodland ASOS observed a peak gust of 66 mph during the event. The high winds also caused blowing dust to occur, which reduced visibilities between one mile to a few hundred yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual October winter storm affected much of south central Kansas on October 26th and 27th. The event started during the early morning hours of Monday, October 26th as a mix of sleet and snow with a second round on Tuesday which consisted of mostly sleet and freezing rain. Locations across Cowley, Chautauqua, Elk and Montogomery counties saw the most significant ice accumulation. The towns of Winfield and especially Arkansas City were hit hard, with numerous reports of widespread power outages along with large trees down. ||Wichita set new snowfall records for both October 26th and 27th. The 1.3 inches on the 26th was the most snow Wichita has ever received that early.  The 1.6 inches from both days combined makes October 2020 the 2nd snowiest October on record for Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter mix prevailed across the county causing minor nuisances for travel.  The precipitation began as freezing drizzle then transitioned over to snow.  Snowfall amounts across the county varied between 1 to 2 inches.  Officially, the 1.3 inches on the 26th was the most snow Wichita had ever received this early in the season.  Secondly, the 1.6 inch storm total made this the 2nd snowiest month on record for the county.  Ice accumulations up to 0.15 inches were noted as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 8-930 p.m. CDT on this Friday evening, a lone severe storm roamed south-southeastward across portions of Osborne County, prompting a few reports of hail up to ping pong ball size. This supercell initiated in north central Osborne County, with the first report of quarter size hail from within the city of Osborne around 8:30 pm. CDT. During the next hour the storm tracked south-southeast through rural portions of the county as hail size increased to 1.5 diameter, with one resident reporting destroyed rose blooms. Around 9:30 p.m. CDT the now-weakening cell exited the county (and this North Central Kansas area altogether). Although additional, more widespread convection developed into the area a few hours later, these later-night storms were not severe. This sunset severe storm fired up slightly behind a surface cold front slipping southward through the region, as weak low level convergence was able to locally overcome fairly strong capping (as evidenced by 700 millibar temperature around 12C). Despite the passing front, surface dewpoints still held up in the upper 60s F near the storm, promoting decent instability as the evening mesoscale environment featured 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 35 knots of deep-layer wind shear. In the mid-upper levels, broad west-northwest flow prevailed over northern Kansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a strong cold front moved across Graham County, high winds gusted between 59 to 81 mph. Around 7:13 pm CDT, Graham County dispatch received a report of an out of control trash burning fire that was burning fields near the historic town of Nicodemus. The fire was close enough to town that the entire town was evacuated for a few hours as thirty five firefighters and 15 trucks responded from multiple counties to stop the fire. The Graham County Fire Department reported the fire was 1.5 miles wide and 2 miles long. The fire occurred to the south and southeast of Nicodemus and extended south to K-18 and east to the Rooks County line. Around 9:30 pm CDT, dispatch reported the fire had moved into Rooks County and was mostly under control or out completely. No injuries or structural damage was reported.

Locations across Cowley, Chautauqua, Elk and Montogomery counties saw the most significant ice accumulation. The towns of Winfield and especially Arkansas City were hit hard, with numerous reports of widespread power outages along with large trees down. ||Wichita set new snowfall records for both October 26th and 27th. The 1.3 inches on the 26th was the most snow Wichita has ever received that early.  The 1.6 inches from both days combined makes October 2020 the 2nd snowiest October on record for Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix occurred across the county.  Snow amounts were generally less than 0.5 inches and ice accumulations up to 0.10 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across southwest Kansas into northern Oklahoma during the late night on December, 2nd, 2020.  Cold air on the north side of the system, led to light snowfall amounts across most of south central Kansas.  Snowfall amounts were 2 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts generally ranged from one to three inches across the county, with the heaviest amounts over western sections of the county.   NWS employees in Wichita, reported two to 2.4 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts generally were one to two inches of snow across the county.  A Coop observer in Belle Plaine reported 1.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system ejected eastward into the central part of the country just after Christmas. While the temperatures at the surface remained well below freezing, temperatures just off the surface remained warm enough to melt any ice that was produced in the cloud. Therefore, there was freezing rain that formed, generally along the HWY 36 corridor, producing numerous power outages and causing several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the day on December 29, a potent winter storm moved into the area. The precipitation started as primarily snow during the morning hours producing a couple inches of accumulation, but switched to freezing rain just before 10 am as warm air aloft moved over the area. Moderate, to at times heavy rain ensued through the rest of the morning and early to middle afternoon hours, before eventually moving out by the evening hours. The main impact from this storm was 10,000 to 20,000 without power in and around the area. Due to the rain rates, not all of the nearly 1 inch of liquid precipitation accreted on surfaces, but a quarter to half inch did accrete, causing a significant disruption to the power, and closing numerous roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to high winds occurred over portions of Cheyenne, Sheridan, and Graham counties as a trough moved slowly across eastern Colorado into western Kansas. A ridge over the eastern United States blocked the low from moving from the western half of the country, which allowed the winds to impact the area for a few hours each day. Something unusual with this event, was the strong winds that occurred during the night Saturday into early Sunday morning. The strong to high winds produced gusts up to 60 mph and helped spread wildfires in Wichita, Norton, and Cheyenne counties. The fires in Wichita and Cheyenne counties were small field fires that were quickly extinguished with no structural damage. The fire in Norton County prompted a State of Emergency and the Kansas Forest Service flew their tanker plane to assist ground units with extinguishing the fire. The grass fire was mainly over open grass and cattle fields; however, an unknown amount of cattle fencing and a cattle pen was burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire just west of the town of Norton prompted the Governor of Kansas to declare a State of Emergency. The fire was originally a ditch fire that strong southerly winds, gusting up to 42 mph, brought out of control.  Kansas Forest Service deployed their tanker airplane to assist the eight ground units responding to the fire. The fire was mainly over open grass and cattle fields; however, an unknown amount of cattle fencing and a cattle pen was burned. It took nearly 8.5 hours to completely extinguish the fire that burned an unknown amount of acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 8-930 p.m. CDT on this Friday evening, a lone severe storm roamed south-southeastward across portions of Osborne County, prompting a few reports of hail up to ping pong ball size. This supercell initiated in north central Osborne County, with the first report of quarter size hail from within the city of Osborne around 8:30 pm. CDT. During the next hour the storm tracked south-southeast through rural portions of the county as hail size increased to 1.5 diameter, with one resident reporting destroyed rose blooms. Around 9:30 p.m. CDT the now-weakening cell exited the county (and this North Central Kansas area altogether). Although additional, more widespread convection developed into the area a few hours later, these later-night storms were not severe. This sunset severe storm fired up slightly behind a surface cold front slipping southward through the region, as weak low level convergence was able to locally overcome fairly strong capping (as evidenced by 700 millibar temperature around 12C). Despite the passing front, surface dewpoints still held up in the upper 60s F near the storm, promoting decent instability as the evening mesoscale environment featured 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 35 knots of deep-layer wind shear. In the mid-upper levels, broad west-northwest flow prevailed over northern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of quarter to ping pong ball size was reported along this swath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arguably the most noteworthy individual thunderstorm of the 2020 convective season within this six-county North Central Kansas area rolled southward across portions of Jewell, Mitchell and mainly eastern Osborne counties on this Friday evening, highlighted by several reports of large to very large hail. Of the counties impacted by this supercell, Jewell was hardest-hit, as copious amounts of tennis ball to softball size hail stones crashed down onto areas in and near the communities of Mankato and Jewell, causing extensive damage to homes, vehicles and area crops. The hail size toned down somewhat farther south into Mitchell and Osborne counties, where limited reports included ping pong ball size stones in Cawker City and golf ball size south of Osborne. Although damaging winds were not a major player during this event, several mesonets clocked gusts of at least 40-50 MPH, and a Kansas Mesonet site near Jewell reached severe-criteria with 65 MPH. Flooding was not much of an issue, with convection staying progressive enough to keep rainfall amounts largely under one inch.||Breaking down event timing and meteorological background, this giant storm invaded northern Jewell County between 7-8 pm. CDT, having already propagated south-southwestward for roughly 90 miles since its inception in east central Nebraska about three hours prior. As it drifted southward into Mitchell County between 8-9 p.m. CDT it started merging with a much smaller, separate storm that had flared up over central Osborne County. Finally, between 9-10 p.m. CDT, the now-merged supercell cluster continued southward while exiting the local area into central Kansas, ending all local storms for the night. This was actually a fairly conditional severe weather setup for North Central Kansas, with various pros and cons for/against robust activity. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided a fair distance south of a vigorous shortwave trough swinging across the Dakotas towards northern MN, resulting in fairly limited forcing aloft. Also working against convection (especially widespread severe) was a plume of warm mid level temperatures, with 700 millibar values of 12-14C promoting a healthy cap. On the other hand, low-level forcing was seasonably-strong, as a well-defined cold front slid southeastward into the area during the evening. Ahead of this boundary, a significantly-unstable airmass awaited, featuring temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F/dewpoints upper 60s-mid 70s F, yielding impressive mixed-layer CAPE up to around 4500 J/kg. This extreme instability, in the presence of sufficient deep-layer wind shear around 35 knots, clearly fostered a mesoscale environment capable of stout convection.
Rainfall amounts for a couple of southern KS counties were quite impressive with totals over 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.00 inches of rain was reported for the previous 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly within an hour either side of midnight CDT on the night of Sunday the 9th into Monday the 10th, a fairly pronounced heat burst (perhaps a series of them) affected Rooks County, prompting both measured/estimated wind gusts up to around 70 MPH. On the south side of Stockton, a mesonet recorded a peak gust of 61 MPH, and there were reports of some minor tree damage in town. Another mesonet station between Palco and Zurich displayed tell-tale signs of a heat burst in its temperature/dewpoint data, as the observation around 9 p.m. CDT was 86/67 F, but closer to midnight had warmed/dried notably to 94/48 F, before gradually stabilizing (cooling/moistening) through the pre-dawn hours. The most telling personal evidence of heat burst activity came from Rooks County State Park, where a man relayed an account of being caught off guard by a low roaring sound followed by several minutes of strong winds he estimated to be at least 70 MPH, which damaged some trees. He noted how unusual the situation was given the lack of obvious thunderstorm activity in the immediate area. From a meteorological standpoint, this was a fairly classic setup for nocturnal heat burst activity. For one, radar data confirmed that Rooks County was underneath high-based, light precipitation, generally 20-60 miles north of a small cluster of thunderstorms drifting east-southeast outside the local area. Mesoscale analysis also confirmed very warm mid level temperatures were present (around 14 C at 700 millibars), along with steep mid level lapse rates. The preceding afternoon of the 9th was quite hot across north central Kansas, with high temperatures well into the mid-upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat burst wind gusts estimated to be as high as 70 MPH resulted in mainly some tree damage in and around the Stockton area, including at Rooks County State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought intense non-thunderstorm and thunderstorm related wind gusts to north central Kansas on the evening of October 11th. This front entered western portions of north central Kansas between 7pm and 8pm CDT. The cold front already had a history of producing severe caliber, non-thunderstorm, straight line winds over western Nebraska and Kansas, and this trend continued along and just behind the cold front as it marched through north central Kansas. There were several reports of wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph across Phillips, Smith, and Osborne Counties. Damage reports from these areas were fairly typical for winds of this magnitude and mainly limited to tree and power line damage. Thunderstorms began to develop along the cold front as it moved farther east into Jewell County between 8:30pm and 9pm CDT. Thunderstorm intensity increased between 9pm CDT and 9:30pm CDT, such that the wind gusts produced by the thunderstorms enhanced the already-high ambient synoptically driven non-thunderstorm wind gusts associated with the cold front. This resulted in additional 60 to 65 mph wind gusts across portions of Jewell County. No specific damage reports were received from this area. Wind gusts decreased as thunderstorms exited Jewell County by 10pm CDT.||As mentioned above, the damaging wind events in the eastern half of north central Kansas were largely driven by convective winds that enhanced already-high ambient winds. Farther west, the severe wind was non-convective and driven by intense pressure rises behind the cold front on the order of 3-5 mb per 2 hrs. This event was somewhat unique due to the narrow overlap, spatially and temporally, of both convective and non-convective damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in tree damage and downed power lines across portions of the county, especially in the town of Osborne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was an active late Friday evening-early Saturday morning across this six-county North Central Kansas area, featuring limited severe weather in Rooks/Phillips counties and some relatively minor flooding mainly within the northeast one-third of Mitchell County. But for the majority of the area this was just a classic, stormy early-summer night (most places measured between 0.50-2.00 inches of rain). As for the handful of severe weather reports, the main issue was a brief burst of intense winds through Rooks County around 2 a.m. CDT, including measured unofficial gusts of 68 MPH north-northeast of Codell, 62 MPH in Stockton and 61 MPH at Plainville (no known damage reports). Hail-wise, the only report featured quarter size stones east of Phillipsburg shortly after 11 p.m. CDT. Regarding heavy rainfall/flooding, primarily northeast Mitchell County was drenched with multiple downpours between midnight-4 a.m. CDT, with several automated gauges and NWS/CoCoRaHS observers tallying 3-5, including 5.05 just northeast of Beloit (unofficial public report of 6 at Simpson). This prompted relatively minor/short-term flash flooding and areal flooding, and also drove the Solomon River out of its banks as the Beloit gauge crested around 22.4 feet on the afternoon of the 27th (flood stage 20 feet). ||Timing-wise, there were basically two main rounds of convection. The first round (mainly 10 p.m. to 2 a.m. CDT) featured generally isolated to scattered coverage, as storms developed directly overhead in response to the onset of a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet (evident at 850 millibars). Then, primarily 2-4 a.m. CDT the next round charged in from the west in the form of a fairly compact linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) that had gotten its act together well before midnight along the KS/CO border. By 4 a.m. CDT, storm intensity had waned considerably across local counties, although mainly stratiform lighter rain held on through around sunrise. This was a fairly typical setup for active nocturnal storms in the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, broad zonal flow reigned as a low amplitude wave rode eastward out of CO during the night. Meanwhile at the surface, a nearly stationary front resided directly through the area in east-northeast to west-southwest fashion, as evening dewpoints averaged mid-60s to around 70 F. Though fairly warm mid-level temps/weak capping held storms at bay until after sunset, the onset of the low level jet easily sparked convection given a healthy reservoir of 2000-3000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE, with the main limiting factor to a more robust severe threat being only modest deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots.

This prompted relatively minor/short-term flash flooding and areal flooding, and also drove the Solomon River out of its banks as the Beloit gauge crested around 22.4 feet on the afternoon of the 27th (flood stage 20 feet). ||Timing-wise, there were basically two main rounds of convection. The first round (mainly 10 p.m. to 2 a.m. CDT) featured generally isolated to scattered coverage, as storms developed directly overhead in response to the onset of a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet (evident at 850 millibars). Then, primarily 2-4 a.m. CDT the next round charged in from the west in the form of a fairly compact linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) that had gotten its act together well before midnight along the KS/CO border. By 4 a.m. CDT, storm intensity had waned considerably across local counties, although mainly stratiform lighter rain held on through around sunrise. This was a fairly typical setup for active nocturnal storms in the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, broad zonal flow reigned as a low amplitude wave rode eastward out of CO during the night. Meanwhile at the surface, a nearly stationary front resided directly through the area in east-northeast to west-southwest fashion, as evening dewpoints averaged mid-60s to around 70 F. Though fairly warm mid-level temps/weak capping held storms at bay until after sunset, the onset of the low level jet easily sparked convection given a healthy reservoir of 2000-3000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE, with the main limiting factor to a more robust severe threat being only modest deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought intense non-thunderstorm and thunderstorm related wind gusts to north central Kansas on the evening of October 11th. This front entered western portions of north central Kansas between 7pm and 8pm CDT. The cold front already had a history of producing severe caliber, non-thunderstorm, straight line winds over western Nebraska and Kansas, and this trend continued along and just behind the cold front as it marched through north central Kansas. There were several reports of wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph across Phillips, Smith, and Osborne Counties. Damage reports from these areas were fairly typical for winds of this magnitude and mainly limited to tree and power line damage. Thunderstorms began to develop along the cold front as it moved farther east into Jewell County between 8:30pm and 9pm CDT. Thunderstorm intensity increased between 9pm CDT and 9:30pm CDT, such that the wind gusts produced by the thunderstorms enhanced the already-high ambient synoptically driven non-thunderstorm wind gusts associated with the cold front. This resulted in additional 60 to 65 mph wind gusts across portions of Jewell County. No specific damage reports were received from this area. Wind gusts decreased as thunderstorms exited Jewell County by 10pm CDT.||As mentioned above, the damaging wind events in the eastern half of north central Kansas were largely driven by convective winds that enhanced already-high ambient winds. Farther west, the severe wind was non-convective and driven by intense pressure rises behind the cold front on the order of 3-5 mb per 2 hrs. This event was somewhat unique due to the narrow overlap, spatially and temporally, of both convective and non-convective damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 MPH was recorded by a mesonet station located 1 mile west of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rounds of strong to marginally-severe thunderstorms affected this six-county North Central Kansas area between the pre-dawn and late afternoon hours on this Sunday. However, this event fell short of forecast expectations in terms of potential coverage/intensity of severe weather, yielding only a few reports of quarter size hail at Ionia (mid-morning) and near Natoma (mid-afternoon). As for how the day played out, this seemed to be a case where factors such as the presence of stubborn early-day convection, along with afternoon development initiating fairly early and in widespread fashion, combined to hinder appreciable airmass destabilization/recovery and thus tempered the magnitude of the potential severe threat (SPC assigned Enhanced Risk to North Central Kansas on Day 1 outlooks).||The first sign of potential complications showed its hand well-before sunrise, as a long-lived round of at least slightly-elevated storms initiated between 2-3 a.m. CDT over Phillips/Rooks/Osborne counties. Over the course of the next 11 hours or so (all the way into early afternoon) this activity evolved into a narrow/broken, north-south oriented band that slid eastward at a snail's pace as new cells continuously regenerated on its western flank. The majority of these storms were sub-severe, producing only small hail, but between 9 a.m. and noon CDT a few cores over Jewell/Mitchell counties pulsed up to severe levels per radar data and the lone ground-truth report of quarters at Ionia. At long last, this early-day convection finally (mostly) exited the local area around 1 p.m. CDT. However, because it had persisted so long into the day, it laid out an outflow boundary/effective warm front near the far southern edges of the local area. Thanks to afternoon heating and meager capping, it did not take long for several strong/severe storms to erupt within a few counties either side of the Interstate 70 corridor between 2-4 p.m. CDT. This resulted in considerable storm coverage across mainly Rooks/Osborne/Mitchell counties, but the only ground-truth severe report was quarter size hail near Natoma. Between 4-6 p.m. CDT, other strong storms moved up into Phillips County from the southwest. However, given that so much convection developed over the local area in a short time, along with the fact that a large severe complex had taken center stage in central/southwestern KS, storm intensity/coverage within North Central Kansas steadily dwindled and the area was all but storm-free by 7 p.m. CDT. Not surprisingly given the forecast expectations, the general synoptic setup appeared rather potent for severe storms. In the mid-upper levels, broad west-northwest flow prevailed over the Central Plains as a series of embedded disturbances rotated around the south side of a large-scale trough/closed low over south central Canada. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided southeast of a quasi-stationary front draped from northwest KS into northern NE. Prior to the eruption of mid-afternoon convection, mixed-layer CAPE reached 2000-4000 J/kg in the presence of 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although storm coverage within this portion of North Central Kansas was quite limited on this Saturday evening, the one legitimate severe storm in the coverage area produced notably large hail up to 3 inches in diameter over far southern Rooks County. This hailer entered the county from the southwest shortly before 8 p.m. CDT, after drifting northeast for a few hours as a left split from activity that initiated near the intersection of Gove/Trego/Ness counties. Between 8-8:20 p.m. CDT, reports of hail ranging from quarter to greater than baseball size occurred along a narrow swath from six miles south-southwest of Plainville up to the south side of Plainville proper. Shortly thereafter, the storm weakened rapidly, with only the faintest radar echoes remaining by 8:45 p.m. CDT. Elsewhere within the local area on this evening, a strong storm tracked southeastward from southwestern Phillips to central Rooks counties, but yielded no ground-truth reports. The initial convective development over west-central Kansas (that eventually spawned the Rooks County hail storm) developed near a somewhat-subtle, north-south oriented surface trough. Forcing aloft was also somewhat subtle, as northern Kansas only received a glancing blow from the southern fringes of a broad mid-level trough focused from central Nebraska northward into the Dakotas. Nonetheless, once storms got going, the environment was certainly supportive of severe activity as mesoscale analysis featured around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and deep layer wind shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from ping pong balls to 3 inches in diameter was reported along this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong and severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front in Nebraska and northern Kansas producing some damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured on wunderground weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 7-10 p.m. CDT on this Monday evening, a broken line of thunderstorms swept across this six-county North Central Kansas area from northwest-to-southeast. The vast majority of this activity was sub-severe, producing only gusty winds as high as 40-50 MPH and brief rainfall. However, one small cell pulsed up just enough to produce a marginally-severe measured gust of 58 MPH near Speed in southwestern Phillips County, shortly before 8 p.m. CDT. Although this fairly low-key line of convection increased in coverage a bit by the time it reached North Central Kansas, its origins could actually be traced back roughly 300 miles to the northwest, to isolated-scattered storms initiating early in the afternoon over southwestern SD and northwestern NE. In the mid-upper levels, a fairly pronounced northwest flow regime ruled the Central Plains, as the region resided within the path of several small-scale disturbances tracking southeastward around the periphery of a larger-scale trough centered over south central Canada. Despite only modest low-level moisture in the wake of a secondary cold front, this convection made the most of steep low-level lapse rates and seasonably-cool mid level temps, with early-evening mesoscale parameters in North Central KS featuring around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 35-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

Regarding heavy rainfall/flooding, primarily northeast Mitchell County was drenched with multiple downpours between midnight-4 a.m. CDT, with several automated gauges and NWS/CoCoRaHS observers tallying 3-5, including 5.05 just northeast of Beloit (unofficial public report of 6 at Simpson). This prompted relatively minor/short-term flash flooding and areal flooding, and also drove the Solomon River out of its banks as the Beloit gauge crested around 22.4 feet on the afternoon of the 27th (flood stage 20 feet). ||Timing-wise, there were basically two main rounds of convection. The first round (mainly 10 p.m. to 2 a.m. CDT) featured generally isolated to scattered coverage, as storms developed directly overhead in response to the onset of a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet (evident at 850 millibars). Then, primarily 2-4 a.m. CDT the next round charged in from the west in the form of a fairly compact linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) that had gotten its act together well before midnight along the KS/CO border. By 4 a.m. CDT, storm intensity had waned considerably across local counties, although mainly stratiform lighter rain held on through around sunrise. This was a fairly typical setup for active nocturnal storms in the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, broad zonal flow reigned as a low amplitude wave rode eastward out of CO during the night. Meanwhile at the surface, a nearly stationary front resided directly through the area in east-northeast to west-southwest fashion, as evening dewpoints averaged mid-60s to around 70 F. Though fairly warm mid-level temps/weak capping held storms at bay until after sunset, the onset of the low level jet easily sparked convection given a healthy reservoir of 2000-3000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE, with the main limiting factor to a more robust severe threat being only modest deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches were reported across the area, with the primary impact being numerous rural roads being covered in water and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the CWA with the surface boundary again along the I-70 corridor initiating a number of severe storms that pushed southeast through the afternoon and evening hours. Severe hail and winds were the primary threat but tornadoes were possible in the QLCS scenario, but none were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outbuildings were destroyed an a pivot irrigation sprinkler was overturned by the high wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It's quite rare that widespread/severe NON-thunderstorm winds take center stage over thunderstorms during June in North Central Kansas, but that's exactly what happened on this Tuesday as an anomalously strong low pressure system (by June standards) advanced through the Central Plains. For sure, the number one weather story across these six counties was several hours of intense north winds, commonly sustained 30-40 MPH and gusting 40-60+ MPH in the wake of a powerful late-spring cold front. Based on a variety of unofficial sensors, some of the highest afternoon-evening gusts featured: 66 MPH near Logan, 62 MPH in rural eastern Rooks County, 59 MPH near Kirwin and 58 MPH at Phillipsburg airport and also near Webber. These winds undoubtedly caused several instances of mainly minor damage within the area, but ground-truth reports were quite limited. For the majority of this area, the overall-worst winds concentrated during portions of the noon-midnight CDT time frame. Turning to thunderstorms, while non-severe activity was common across much of North Central Kansas during the late afternoon and especially evening, this area narrowly dodged a severe storm threat due mainly to a strong surface cold front and dryline passing through prior to robust development taking place along these boundaries. However, a few strong to severe storms materialized just barely off to the east-northeast within counties such as Republic KS and Thayer NE. ||Both in the mid-upper levels and at the surface, the intensity of this weather system was quite abnormal for June...more reminiscent of those that sometimes occur earlier in the spring. Aloft, a deep closed low slowly tracked from eastern CO around daybreak on the 9th to northeast KS by sunset, eventually departing the Central Plains into northern MO by sunrise on the 10th. The associated surface low center lumbered east through northern KS over the course of the day (nearly directly across the local area), deepening to around 993 millibars in the process. While the position and track of the surface low/cold front spared most of the area from severe storms, especially Jewell/Mitchell counties dodged a potentially significant threat as the environment briefly became conditionally supportive of supercells and possible tornadoes during the afternoon. In fact, mesoscale parameters in this area briefly featured a potent combination of mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg, deep layer wind shear of 50-70 knots and 0-1 kilometer shear of 20-30 knots. As for the unusual nature of such intense northerly winds so late in the spring, it's worth noting that howling speeds of 50-70 knots a few thousand feet above the ground (at 850 millibars) registered 3-4 standard deviations above normal for the date...basically off the charts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was recorded at 9:59 PM CDT by a mesonet station located 9 miles north-northeast of Codell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the CWA bringing strong winds behind it, all non-thunderstorm induced. The gradient was really tight aided by the tropical disturbance remnants making its way through Missouri. Gusts as high as 78 mph were reported with many reports over the 58 mph threshold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed at a location 4 NE Hayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rogue storm developed across the far northern sections of central Kansas and ultimately transformed into a squall line that moved eastward across the remaining sections of central and portions of south central Kansas.  Of note, was the widespread heavy rain that fell across much of the area. Rainfall amounts were consistently in the 1.25 to 3.5 inch range for many locations.  The sites with rainfall amounts higher than 3 inches were individually added.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 33 mph was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the area but with minimal instability, the result was only one severe weather report.

A weak shortwave trough moved across the CWA under weak flow and interacted with a boundary draped across the I-70 corridor to initiate thunderstorms along HWY 283 to Dodge City. Sufficient CAPE and daytime heating sparked a number of storms that contained severe hail up to the size of golf balls in some locations.

An unseasonably strong low pressure system moved across potions of central and south central Kansas during the late evening hours or June 9th and the early morning hours of June 10th, 2020. Tree limbs were knocked down across central and south central Kansas, with most of the limbs 5 to 6 inches in diameter.  Some power pole were also knocked down.  The winds gusted as high as 59 mph at Wichita Eisenhower National airport, with numerous reports of tree damage in Wichita, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust was reported in Westfall, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 9 p.m. and midnight CDT on this Wednesday evening, a smattering of mostly-weak thunderstorms rumbled from west-to-east across portions of this six-county North Central Kansas area, as the vast majority of severe storms in the region focused at least slightly off to the north and east. However, between 10:30-11:15 p.m. CDT, one small storm cluster flared up to severe levels over Jewell County, yielding a lone ground-truth report of measured 68 MPH winds in the far northeast corner of the county near Webber. Radar also indicated that this storm might have produced severe-criteria hail over mainly central sections of the county, but there were no verifying reports. In the mid-upper levels, this was not a strongly-forced event, with the main feature consisting of a low-amplitude shortwave trough tracking east-southeast across Nebraska out of western SD over the course of the afternoon-evening. Surface features in North Central Kansas during the evening were fairly nondescript, dominated by weak high pressure and generally southerly breezes around 10 MPH or less. However, fairly steep low-mid level lapse rates were in place, as evening mesoscale analysis revealed around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 35 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the CWA bringing strong winds behind it, all non-thunderstorm induced. The gradient was really tight aided by the tropical disturbance remnants making its way through Missouri. Gusts as high as 78 mph were reported with many reports over the 58 mph threshold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed at the Cimarron National Grasslands mesonet station.

Damaging wind was the main threat with ample DCAPE usually associated in MCS like events.

A surface boundary continued to be draped across northern counties with a weak upper level disturbance pushing out of Colorado. No severe weather occurred but a watch along with a slight risk was issued for these areas of west central to north central Kansas. Gusty winds up to 40-50 mph along with hail up to pennies occurred but no severe weather reports were reported.

A rogue storm developed across the far northern sections of central Kansas and ultimately transformed into a squall line that moved eastward across the remaining sections of central and portions of south central Kansas.  Of note, was the widespread heavy rain that fell across much of the area. Rainfall amounts were consistently in the 1.25 to 3.5 inch range for many locations.  The sites with rainfall amounts higher than 3 inches were individually added.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low tracked across the Texas panhandle into northwestern Oklahoma that produced snow on the backside across southern counties of the CWA along the border. Reports up to a foot in the warning areas of Meade to Barber County with lesser amounts for the next tier of counties west and north of this line. Coupled with strong north winds gusting up to 30-40 mph, substantial impacts in the warning and advisory areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was observed in Ashland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northwestern portion of Kansas saw non-thunderstorm high sustained winds and gusts associated with a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours of December 22nd that continued until the afternoon hours of December 23rd. The Goodland, KS ASOS recorded both the highest sustained winds at 46 mph and the highest wind gust at 66 mph. These winds caused damage to property as well as reduced visibilities in the area due to blowing dust and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Behind a cold front, high winds up to 47 mph sustained and wind gusts up to 61 mph were observed in Sherman County from the afternoon of 12/22/20 through the morning of 12/23/20. Both the max sustained winds of 47 mph and the max wind gust of 66 mph were recorded at the Goodland, KS ASOS. The high winds also caused blowing snow which reduced visibility to 1/4 of a mile during the morning of 12/23/20. Key impacts: blowing snow, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of light wintry precipitation set up near the CO/KS border on December 2nd. Light snow was seen during the morning hours that transitioned to freezing drizzle later in the day. This precipitation caused hazardous road conditions that led to a vehicle accident in Sherman County, KS during the evening hours. The reported morning snowfall totals ranged from 0.1-0.5 with 0.03-0.04 of liquid equivalent precipitation for the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported an accident via social media involving a jack knifed semi truck on I-70 at mile marker 9 due to slick roads caused by freezing drizzle and light snow in the county. The report was received at 6:55 PM on 12/2/20. Key impacts: traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of December 27th, two large bands of snow developed in Colorado and moved into northwest Kansas. The northern most band formed over northeast Colorado and gradually moved south and east along the Kansas-Nebraska-Colorado border. The second band formed over southeast Colorado and slowly moved north into Greeley and Wichita counties before eventually merging with the northern band. Throughout the multi-day event, numerous small bands developed and moved across northwest Kansas producing snow that ranged in depth from a trace to 6 inches, with amounts increasing from south to north across the area. Light freezing drizzle produced a glaze of ice in Logan County. The highest snowfall totals occurred in northwest Sheridan County. Injuries were reported with accidents in Wichita, Logan, and Gove counties. Multiple non-injury accidents also occurred across Thomas, Logan, and Gove counties. Numerous vehicle slide-offs were also reported across Sherman, Thomas, and Cheyenne counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow produced light snow accumulations across Cheyenne County ranging from 1 to 2 inches. At 6:58 pm CST on December 28th, a semi-truck traveling north-bound on KS-27 near the Kansas-Nebraska state line slid at the base of a hill due to snow-packed roads. The semi blocked both lanes of traffic for four hours with police units from Dundy County, Nebraska and Cheyenne County, Kansas responding to the situation. No injuries were reported. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although parts of this six-county North Central Kansas area received a very light dusting of snow on this Wednesday, it managed to avoid the steadier snow and blizzard conditions that slammed parts of Nebraska only 50-100 miles to the north. However, it could not avoid the same feisty northwest winds that its northern neighbor endured, as much of the day featured sustained speeds commonly 30-40 MPH and gusts of 45-60+ MPH. While observations from nearby/surrounding counties strongly suggest that all six local counties observed at least sporadic/brief instances of sustained 40 MPH and/or 58+ MPH gusts, the only verified severe-criteria gusts within the area included 61 MPH at the Phillipsburg airport and 58 MPH at Rooks County Regional Airport. There were no known ground-truth damage reports. ||In the mid-upper levels, the main driving force behind this high wind event was the passage of a deep, slightly-positively-tilted trough, with its primary vorticity maximum tracking from the Central Rockies on the evening of the 22nd, and then across the heart of Kansas-Nebraska during the day on the 23rd before departing into the Upper Midwest/Upper Great Lakes through the evening-overnight. At the surface, the associated low pressure center gradually intensified from around 994 millibars as it crossed North Central Kansas during the overnight-early morning of the 22nd-23rd, to 988 millibars by mid-day in the IA-MN-WI border area, and then maintained intensity while reaching Lake Superior overnight. Locally, the initial surge of strong northwest winds passed through between midnight-3 a.m. behind the main synoptic cold front, but gusts largely peaked around 50 MPH or less. The overall-strongest winds of the day then occurred several hours later between mid-morning and early-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for much of the day the west-to-east gradient across the state of Kansas averaged 12-15 millibars). Between 5-7 p.m. wind gusts diminished to around 30 MPH or less, and they further decreased through the remainder of the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic gusts near 60 MPH and sustained speeds near 40 MPH occurred across the county. The peak gust of the day was 61 MPH, which was recorded at 9:53 AM and 12:53 PM by the Phillipsburg Airport AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of December 27th, two large bands of snow developed in Colorado and moved into northwest Kansas. The northern most band formed over northeast Colorado and gradually moved south and east along the Kansas-Nebraska-Colorado border. The second band formed over southeast Colorado and slowly moved north into Greeley and Wichita counties before eventually merging with the northern band. Throughout the multi-day event, numerous small bands developed and moved across northwest Kansas producing snow that ranged in depth from a trace to 6 inches, with amounts increasing from south to north across the area. Light freezing drizzle produced a glaze of ice in Logan County. The highest snowfall totals occurred in northwest Sheridan County. Injuries were reported with accidents in Wichita, Logan, and Gove counties. Multiple non-injury accidents also occurred across Thomas, Logan, and Gove counties. Numerous vehicle slide-offs were also reported across Sherman, Thomas, and Cheyenne counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple snow bands moved across Wichita county, producing only a trace of snow. While snow accumulations were low, roads became slick. At 8:07 am CST on December 29th, a passenger vehicle accident was reported on KS-25 near mile marker 118. One person was injured. Key impacts: traffic accident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across Logan County during the event, producing a range of snow from a trace to 1.8 inches. The Logan County Emergency Manager also reported freezing drizzle that accumulated a glaze of ice on surfaces. Several slide-offs occurred on I-70 along with two accidents. At 7:49 pm CST on December 28th, a rollover accident occurred on US-40 between County Roads 360 and 370 with a passenger vehicle containing five passengers. Three injuries were reported. At 11:36 pm CST on December 28th, a 2 passenger vehicle collision occurred on I-70 near mile marker 79. No injuries were reported with the second accident. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northwestern portion of Kansas saw non-thunderstorm high sustained winds and gusts associated with a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours of December 22nd that continued until the afternoon hours of December 23rd. The Goodland, KS ASOS recorded both the highest sustained winds at 46 mph and the highest wind gust at 66 mph. These winds caused damage to property as well as reduced visibilities in the area due to blowing dust and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing snow caused by high winds was observed via Kansas Department of Highways camera during the afternoon of 12/23/20. The blowing snow reduced visibility down to an estimated 1/4 of a mile. Key impacts: hazardous traveling conditions, blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to snow with minor accumulations reported. Ice accumulations that continued into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages on the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain overspread the county during the evening hours of December 31. Public reports of estimated ice accumulations were around .10 to .15 inches, with the higher amounts across the northern sections of the county. This storm continued into January 1, 2021.

At 5:28 pm CST on December 28th, a rollover accident involving a passenger vehicle occurred near mile marker 99 on I-70. No injuries were reported. At 6:05 pm CST on December 28th, a two semi-truck collision occurred resulting in one injury and a truck stuck in a ditch. The accident occurred near mile marker 99 on I-70. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved northeast across northwest Kansas producing snow ranging in amount from a trace to 2 inches. The highest snow accumulations occurred in Sherman, Wallace, and Decatur counties. Vehicle slide-offs were reported in Thomas, Sherman, and Sheridan counties due to the slick road conditions. Two no-injury accidents were reported in Sherman County near the Kansas-Colorado state line the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow accumulated across Sherman County ranging in amounts from 1 to 2 inches. Two accidents with no injuries occurred near the Colorado-Kansas state line on I-70 around 6:15 am MST. The first accident was a rollover van. The second accident was a semi-truck that jack-knifed into a ditch. A passenger vehicle spin-out also occurred on I-70 near mile marker 31 around 6:20 am MST due to slick roads. No injuries were reported. Key impacts: traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A batch of strong winds moved across central and south central Kansas during the nighttime hours of December 22nd-23rd with speeds ranging from 50-55mph. Another round of strong winds occurred during the afternoon at a few sites with speeds peaking at 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Salina Regional Airport reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite near to slightly above normal precipitation occurring over Northwest Kansas, the D2 Severe to D3 Extreme Drought status did not change for December.  Precipitation amounts ranged from 0.25 to 1 which is near normal for December.  Counties in the D2 Severe Drought include Wichita, Sheridan, the southeastern half of Logan and Norton counties, and the northwestern half of Graham.  The rest of Northwest Kansas was in D3 Extreme Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation across the county ranged from 0.50 to 1.  This was near the normal precipitation range of 0.25 to 1.  Key impacts:  drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During December, portions of three of the six North Central Kansas counties remained in Severe Drought (D2)-or-worse per weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, as overall there was very little change to local drought categories during the month despite prevailing below normal precipitation (not unusual for a winter month outside the growing season). More specifically, D2 remained intact across much of northern Phillips and Smith counties along with far northwestern Jewell County, while Extreme Drought (D3) lingered within the extreme northwest edge of Phillips County. Outside of the D2/D3 areas, the remainder of the six-county area was a varied mix of Moderate Drought (D1) and Abnormally Dry (D0), as portions of Jewell, Mitchell, eastern Osborne and southeastern Smith counties actually improved to D0 early in the month (effectively reverting back to what was assigned there in late-October). ||Going into more detail regarding December precipitation trends, it was a fairly dry month across North Central Kansas, marking the fourth month out of the last five to feature prevailing below normal precipitation across most of the area (November being the only somewhat-wetter exception in some spots). The majority of NWS cooperative observer/CoCoRaHS stations only reported between 0.15-0.40 for the month (or generally 25-50 percent of normal). Measured extremes ranged from as much as 0.49 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit and 0.46 in Beloit, to as little as 0.09 near Damar and 0.12 in Smith Center. More significantly, and from a longer-duration perspective covering the five-month-stretch from August-December, a few of the very-driest NWS observer precipitation totals within/near the D3/D2 area included 2.02 at Logan (driest on record), 2.07 at Phillipsburg (driest on record) and 2.71 in Smith Center (2nd-driest on record). Temperature-wise, although certainly not as big of a drought-factor as it would have been during the growing season, December was the second-straight warmer-than-average month, with most local stations averaging 5-6 degrees F above normal.

Counties in the D2 Severe Drought include Wichita, Sheridan, the southeastern half of Logan and Norton counties, and the northwestern half of Graham.  The rest of Northwest Kansas was in D3 Extreme Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation across the county ranged from 0.25 to 0.50.  This was near the normal precipitation range of 0.50 to 1.  Key impacts:  drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms that developed in eastern Colorado moved across the border into NW Kansas and continued eastward during the late afternoon hours. The thunderstorms produced hail up to a quarter size and wind gusts up to 69 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station reported a 61 mph wind gust at location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably strong winds occurred for a third straight day.  The majority of the high winds occurred along the Colorado/Kansas border, with more sporadic damage and/or high wind gusts to the east.  Noteworthy damage reports include three inch diameter tree limbs being blown down in St. Francis which may have also caused power poles to be broken, the high winds pushing two semis together on I-70 near Kanorado, and a truck being flipped that was towing a trailer near Lenora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of over 60 MPH occurred across the county.  The high winds also caused some shingles to be blown off a house, and a one inch diameter tree limb to be blown down in St. Francis.  In addition, three inch diameter tree limbs were blown down in St. Francis, which may have lead to power poles being broken as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As part of a larger severe weather event some tree damage occurred in Leavenworth, Kansas due to strong thunderstorm winds. More damage occurred east of this area into western Missouri. The next night a similar event took place, with late evening and overnight thunderstorms producing some damage further east into Missouri, but a lone report of wind damage came in from the Kansas side.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a tree down on a house in Leavenworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed in eastern Colorado moved across the border into northwestern Kansas and continued to move east into the overnight hours of June 26th. Thunderstorms contained wind gusts up to an estimated 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station KKSBOGUE1 reported a 73 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unorganized supercells formed along the dry line in Wallace/Logan counties and moved east into an increasingly unstable air mass. As the cells moved east, they merged into a line that extended from Greeley County to Sheridan County. The storms from this event produced a landspout tornado near Hoxie, KS, up to ping pong sized hail, and flash flooding in Grinnell, KS with a vehicle stuck in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm Chaster reported ping pong ball sized hail falling on US 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed off the Rockies in a line north of I-70 with a few clusters of storms to the south of I-70. As the storms moved east into Kansas, they produced wind gusts up to 70 mph causing blowing dust to reduce visibility in Rawlins County. The storms also produced up to penny sized hail in Greeley County.
